title: Introduction to Large Language Models
description: Understand how modern LLMs work from the inside out — from self-attention and Transformer architecture through training, alignment, prompting, RAG, and fine-tuning.

lessons.title: How Language Models Work
lessons.content:
# How Language Models Work

A language model looks like it’s “thinking in sentences,” but under the hood it’s doing something almost embarrassingly simple:

> given some text, predict the **next token**.

That’s it. And yet, when you scale this objective up with lots of data and a big neural network, the model is forced to internalize grammar, facts, style, and even some patterns that look like reasoning.

![Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)](/api/wiki-images/pre-training/images/huyenchip-2023-05-02-rlhf-html_002.gif)

## Tokens: the model’s “alphabet”

A **token** is a chunk of text (often a word piece). Models don’t predict characters or words directly; they predict token IDs from a vocabulary.

So the model sees something like:

```
"The cat sat on the"  ->  [314, 9821, 6712, 389, 262]
```

and learns to output a probability distribution over the next token.

## Next-token prediction = a probability model over text

A **language model (LM)** assigns probabilities to sequences by multiplying conditional probabilities:

\[
P(x_1,\dots,x_T) = \prod_{t=1}^T P(x_t \mid x_{<t})
\]

Training is: adjust weights so the true next token gets high probability. In practice we minimize cross-entropy loss (negative log-likelihood).

A key detail: the model doesn’t output probabilities directly. It outputs **logits** (raw scores), then applies **softmax** to turn them into probabilities. Implementations use a numerically stable softmax (shift by the max logit) to avoid overflow.

## Autoregressive generation: how a model “writes”

At inference time, we do **autoregressive generation**:

1. Feed the prompt tokens.
2. Get a distribution for the next token.
3. Pick a token (sampling or greedy).
4. Append it to the context.
5. Repeat.

That loop is why small changes early can cascade into very different continuations.

## Sampling knobs: temperature and top-p

If we always pick the most likely token, outputs can be repetitive. Sampling makes text more diverse.

- **Temperature** rescales logits before softmax.
  - Low temperature (< 1): sharper distribution, more deterministic.
  - High temperature (> 1): flatter distribution, more surprising.

- **Top-p sampling (nucleus sampling)**: keep the smallest set of tokens whose cumulative probability ≥ *p*, then sample only from that set. This avoids wasting probability mass on the long tail of bizarre tokens.

Here’s the core idea in pseudocode:

```python
logits = model(prompt_tokens)
logits = logits / temperature
probs = softmax(logits)

# top-p
sorted_tokens = sort_by_prob_desc(probs)
keep = smallest_prefix_with_cumprob_at_least_p(sorted_tokens, p=0.9)
next_token = sample_from(keep)
```

## Perplexity: “how surprised is the model?”

**Perplexity** is a common evaluation metric for next-token models. It’s essentially the exponentiated average negative log probability:

\[
\text{ppl} = \exp\left( -\frac{1}{T}\sum_{t=1}^T \log P(x_t \mid x_{<t}) \right)
\]

Intuition: perplexity is like the model’s effective branching factor. If perplexity is 20, it’s as if the model is, on average, choosing among ~20 plausible next tokens.

## Why this simple objective becomes powerful

To predict the next token well, the model must compress a lot of regularities:

- syntax (what can follow grammatically)
- semantics (what makes sense)
- world knowledge (what’s typically true)
- discourse (what’s consistent with earlier context)

And because Transformers can use self-attention to pull relevant information from anywhere in the context, they can condition on long-range dependencies instead of just the last few words.

## Summary

Language models are trained to predict the next token, and generation is just repeating that prediction step autoregressively. Temperature and top-p control how we sample from the model’s next-token distribution, while perplexity measures how well the model predicts text.

## Recommended Reading

- [Language Models are Few-Shot Learners](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.14165) — Scaling next-token prediction leads to surprisingly broad capabilities.
- [The Transformer Family (v2)](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-01-27-the-transformer-family-v2/) — Clear, structured explanation of Transformer components that power modern LMs.
- [Numerically Stable Softmax and Cross Entropy](https://jaykmody.com/blog/stable-softmax/) — Why softmax needs care in real floating-point code.
- [nanoGPT](https://github.com/karpathy/nanoGPT) — Minimal, readable codebase for training a GPT-like model end-to-end.
lessons.summary: Language models learn by next-token prediction, and generating text is just repeating that prediction step autoregressively. Sampling methods like temperature and top-p shape creativity, while perplexity quantifies how well the model predicts sequences.

image_metadata.caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context

image_metadata.caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches training Memorizing Transformer from scratch. (Wu et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the Memorizing Transformer to highlight its practical advantage; use to motivate why fine-tuning with memory augmentation is a viable and efficient strategy
image_metadata.description: Line graph comparing perplexity vs training steps for: training a memorizing transformer from scratch, fine-tuning a vanilla Transformer with k-NN memory added, and a baseline vanilla Transformer, showing fine-tuning achieves similar performance to training from scratch

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi extrapolates better than sinusoidal, rotary, and T5 encodings. (Press et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing ALiBi or comparing positional encoding schemes; use to motivate why additive linear biases outperform learned or sinusoidal encodings for out-of-distribution sequence lengths.
image_metadata.description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary, T5 relative, ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve as context length grows beyond training length.

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization

image_metadata.caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens

image_metadata.caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years

image_metadata.caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
image_metadata.description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields

image_metadata.caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases

image_metadata.caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with calculus/ML background who benefit from seeing loss landscape shapes
image_metadata.description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's labeler UI: rank responses 1–7 and by preference to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic and what it implies about subjectivity in human feedback
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of the actual web UI used by OpenAI labelers to rank InstructGPT responses, showing a prompt, multiple model-generated responses, a 1–7 rating scale, and a ranking interface

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map, and revisit when explaining why the RL phase needs a KL penalty to stay close to the SFT checkpoint.
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) pretraining, (2) supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and (3) RLHF with a reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after students understand the three-phase pipeline; use it when explaining why unconstrained reward maximization leads to reward hacking and how the KL term prevents degenerate outputs.
image_metadata.description: The mathematical objective function used in RLHF, showing the combined loss as a sum of the reward model score expectation and a KL divergence penalty term that constrains the RL-tuned policy to stay close to the SFT model.

image_metadata.caption: Schulman's proposed RL-based fixes for LLM hallucination. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the gap between theoretical expectations and empirical results of RLHF; use it to prompt critical thinking about whether RL can reliably teach models to express uncertainty.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs 'know what they know' and that RL-based methods could force models to only assert information they are confident about.

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT data: RLHF increases hallucination despite higher overall preference. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing RLHF benefits to immediately introduce nuance; ideal for discussions on evaluation metrics, reward hacking, and why optimizing for human preference does not guarantee factual correctness.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart or comparison graphic from the InstructGPT paper showing that RLHF-trained models exhibit higher hallucination rates than SFT-only models, despite being preferred overall by human labelers across other quality dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Full LLM training pipeline: pre-training through post-training alignment. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM training to orient students before introducing specific stages; return to it as a navigation aid when transitioning between pre-training and post-training topics.
image_metadata.description: A horizontal pipeline diagram showing the full LLM development and training workflow, from data collection through pre-training, continued pre-training, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and alignment/post-training stages, with annotations highlighting new methodologies covered in the article.

image_metadata.caption: MMLU scores for recent open-weight LLMs; higher is better. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Surface when discussing how to evaluate LLMs or when comparing Qwen 2 to other models; useful for teaching students to critically read benchmark tables and understand what MMLU measures.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing MMLU benchmark scores across the latest open-weight LLMs (including Qwen 2 variants and contemporaries), with model names on the x-axis and MMLU accuracy scores on the y-axis, sourced directly from official research papers.

image_metadata.caption: Qwen 2 pre-training techniques including two-stage continued pre-training. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when teaching data-centric approaches to pre-training or when contrasting Qwen 2's pipeline with other models; ideal for a discussion on why data quality matters as much as quantity.
image_metadata.description: A structured summary table or diagram listing the key techniques used in Qwen 2 pre-training, including data filtering, data mixing, synthetic data generation via prior Qwen models, multi-task instruction data inclusion, and a two-stage pre-training approach (standard pre-training followed by long-context continued pre-training).

image_metadata.caption: Typical LLM development flow: pre-training → SFT → alignment. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce before explaining DPO or SFT in detail; also effective when students ask 'why can't you just use the base model?' to show the role of post-training stages.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the typical LLM development flow: starting from a base pre-trained model, moving through supervised fine-tuning (SFT), then alignment via reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) or direct preference optimization (DPO), resulting in a chat/instruction-following model.

image_metadata.caption: Qwen 2 post-training: offline then online DPO with rejection sampling. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when teaching the difference between offline and online RL/DPO methods, or when discussing how preference datasets are constructed; pairs well with the DPO overview diagram (Image 9).
image_metadata.description: A summary diagram or table of Qwen 2 post-training techniques, covering the two-phase alignment approach: offline DPO on an existing dataset followed by online DPO with a reward model using rejection sampling, plus details on dataset construction combining human labeling and synthetic annotation.

image_metadata.caption: DPO alignment: training LLMs to prefer chosen over rejected responses. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing DPO to students, especially before or after a mathematical derivation; also useful as a reference when students encounter DPO mentioned in multiple model papers throughout the article.
image_metadata.description: A detailed overview diagram of Direct Preference Optimization (DPO) for LLM alignment, likely showing the training objective, the role of chosen vs. rejected responses, the reference model, and how the policy model is updated to prefer human-preferred outputs over rejected ones.

image_metadata.caption: Apple AFM's 3-stage pre-training pipeline with data reweighting. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when comparing pre-training strategies across different organizations, or when teaching the concept of training curricula and how data composition shifts between pre-training stages.
image_metadata.description: A three-step pre-training pipeline diagram for Apple's AFM (Apple Foundation Models), showing: (1) core pre-training on 6.3 trillion tokens, (2) continued pre-training with down-weighted web-crawl data and up-weighted math/code data, and (3) a final context-extension or specialization stage.

image_metadata.caption: Knowledge distillation: student (3B) learns from teacher (6.4B) soft labels. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing knowledge distillation — use it to explain why distillation provides a richer training signal than standard cross-entropy on hard labels alone.
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing two training approaches: (a) standard pre-training using cross-entropy loss on training tokens, and (b) knowledge distillation where a 3B student model learns from both training tokens and soft labels from a 6.4B teacher model, with a convex combination loss (0.9 weight on teacher labels).

image_metadata.caption: AFM pre-training pipeline: core training, distillation, and context lengthening. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a wrap-up after covering each AFM pre-training stage, or as an overview before diving into individual stages to orient students.
image_metadata.description: Summary table or diagram listing the techniques used across the Apple Foundation Model (AFM) pre-training pipeline, including core pre-training, continued pre-training, and context lengthening stages.

image_metadata.caption: AFM post-training: iTeC combines SFT, DPO, IPO, and online RL. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching post-training alignment, especially to contrast single-algorithm approaches with Apple's committee-based iterative method.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of the Apple Foundation Model (AFM) post-training pipeline, covering supervised fine-tuning (SFT), rejection sampling, preference tuning methods (DPO, IPO, online RL), and the iTeC committee-based iterative approach with a reward model.

image_metadata.caption: Gemma 2 pre-training: distillation for small models, scratch training for 27B. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing pre-training strategies across model families, or when discussing why knowledge distillation is preferred over simply scaling data for smaller models.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of Gemma 2 pre-training techniques, highlighting knowledge distillation for smaller models (27B trained from scratch; smaller models distilled), training data scale, and the rationale that small models are often undertrained.

image_metadata.caption: Gemma 2 post-training: oversized reward model and WARP averaging. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing RLHF reward model design choices, or when contrasting Gemma 2's post-training with Apple's iTeC approach.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of Gemma 2 post-training techniques, including SFT with teacher-generated responses (knowledge distillation during fine-tuning), RLHF with a reward model 10x larger than the policy model, and WARP model averaging.

image_metadata.caption: MMLU scores: Llama 3.1 405B approaches GPT-4 level performance. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Llama 3.1 to motivate why its training approach matters, or when teaching how benchmark results relate to model size and training choices.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart showing MMLU benchmark scores for multiple LLM models including Llama 3.1 variants (8B, 70B, 405B), GPT-4, and others, illustrating how Llama 3.1 405B approaches or matches frontier model performance.

image_metadata.caption: Llama 3.1 pre-training: 3 stages ending with high-quality annealing data. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching data curriculum strategies in pre-training, especially to illustrate the outsized impact of final-stage annealing on specific benchmarks.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of Llama 3.1 pre-training pipeline across three stages: initial pre-training on 15T tokens, continued pre-training with adjusted data mix (~800B tokens), and annealing on high-quality data (including GSM8K and MATH) for the final stage.

image_metadata.caption: Llama 3.1 post-training: iterative SFT+DPO rounds with rejection sampling. (Raschka/Meta)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching DPO-based alignment pipelines, especially to contrast with PPO/RLHF and to demonstrate why iterative rounds improve stability.
image_metadata.description: Annotated figure from the Llama 3.1 technical paper showing the iterative post-training procedure: multiple rounds of SFT and DPO, rejection sampling using a reward model, and incorporation of both human and synthetic data across rounds.

image_metadata.caption: Llama 3.1 post-training: SFT, DPO, rejection sampling, and model averaging. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a summary after covering Llama 3.1 post-training details, or to compare with AFM and Gemma 2 post-training approaches side by side.
image_metadata.description: Summary diagram of Llama 3.1 post-training techniques, covering SFT, rejection sampling, DPO, reward model training, and model averaging/merging applied to SFT, DPO, and reward models across training rounds.

image_metadata.caption: Pre-training technique comparison across Qwen 2, AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the pre-training phase of LLM development, especially when contrasting how different organizations structure their training pipelines and what steps they share.
image_metadata.description: A comparison table or visual overview showing pre-training techniques used across four LLMs: Qwen 2, Apple AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3, highlighting shared and distinct methods such as multi-stage pre-training, context lengthening, and annealing steps.

image_metadata.caption: Post-training technique comparison across Qwen 2, AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3. (Raschka)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing LLM alignment and post-training strategies, particularly to illustrate the diversity of approaches and prompt discussion about trade-offs between DPO and RLHF.
image_metadata.description: A comparison table or visual overview showing post-training techniques used across four LLMs: Qwen 2, Apple AFM, Gemma 2, and Llama 3, covering methods such as rejection sampling, DPO, RLHF, and supervised fine-tuning stages.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau et al. (2014): Attention mechanism added to RNN encoder-decoder.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing attention mechanisms before covering transformers; use to contrast RNN-based attention with the self-attention in transformers.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the attention mechanism added to a recurrent neural network (RNN) for sequence-to-sequence tasks, showing how attention weights connect encoder hidden states to decoder outputs for neural machine translation.

image_metadata.caption: Vaswani et al. (2017): Original Transformer encoder-decoder architecture diagram.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the central reference diagram when teaching the transformer architecture; revisit when discussing BERT (encoder-only) and GPT (decoder-only) as architectural derivatives.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the original Transformer model from 'Attention Is All You Need', showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right) with multi-head attention blocks, feed-forward layers, positional encoding, and residual connections.

image_metadata.caption: Devlin et al. (2018): BERT pre-training with masked language modeling and fine-tuning.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining encoder-style LLMs and the bifurcation between encoder (BERT) and decoder (GPT) architectures; use to contrast with GPT's unidirectional pretraining.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from the BERT paper illustrating pre-training objectives (Masked Language Modeling and Next Sentence Prediction) and fine-tuning on downstream tasks, showing bidirectional context flow through the transformer encoder.

image_metadata.caption: Radford & Narasimhan (2018): GPT decoder-only architecture with pretraining and fine-tuning.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the BERT diagram when teaching the encoder vs. decoder architectural split; use as a foundation before introducing GPT-2, GPT-3, and InstructGPT.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from the original GPT paper showing the decoder-only transformer architecture and the two-stage approach: unsupervised pretraining via next-word prediction followed by supervised fine-tuning on downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Lewis et al. (2019): BART combines encoder-decoder for denoising sequence-to-sequence pretraining.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after covering BERT and GPT to illustrate how BART synthesizes both paradigms; useful when discussing trade-offs between encoder-only, decoder-only, and encoder-decoder architectures.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from the BART paper comparing BART's encoder-decoder denoising pretraining with other models (BERT, GPT, etc.), showing how corrupted input text is reconstructed by the full sequence-to-sequence architecture.

image_metadata.caption: Dao et al. (2022): FlashAttention uses IO-aware tiling for fast, memory-efficient attention.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing efficiency improvements to the transformer; use to motivate why hardware-aware algorithm design matters for scaling LLMs.
image_metadata.description: Diagram from the FlashAttention paper illustrating the IO-aware tiled computation approach for computing exact attention efficiently, contrasting memory access patterns between standard attention and FlashAttention on GPU hardware.

image_metadata.caption: Geiping & Goldstein (2022): BERT trained on one GPU in 24h reveals efficiency trade-offs.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing scaling laws and compute budgets; use to challenge students' intuitions about the relationship between model size, training time, and performance.
image_metadata.description: Results figure from the Cramming paper showing performance (e.g., GLUE scores) of a BERT-style model trained on a single GPU for 24 hours, compared against baselines, with analysis of training efficiency trade-offs.

image_metadata.caption: Lialin et al. (2022): Taxonomy of 40+ parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing fine-tuning strategies for LLMs; use as a reference map when students encounter specific PEFT methods like LoRA or adapters in later readings.
image_metadata.description: Taxonomy diagram from the PEFT survey categorizing over 40 parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods into groups such as prefix tuning, adapters, and low-rank adaptation (LoRA), with comparisons of their properties.

image_metadata.caption: Hoffmann et al. (2022): Chinchilla scaling law — training tokens matter as much as model size.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching scaling laws; use to prompt discussion on why many contemporary LLMs were undertrained and how this insight changed subsequent model development.
image_metadata.description: Figures from the Chinchilla paper showing the relationship between model size, number of training tokens, and loss, illustrating the linear scaling law and demonstrating that Chinchilla (70B parameters, 1.4T tokens) outperforms GPT-3 (175B parameters, 300B tokens).

image_metadata.caption: Ouyang et al. (2022): InstructGPT three-step RLHF pipeline for aligning GPT-3.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing LLM alignment and RLHF; use as the primary reference diagram before discussing Constitutional AI and other alignment approaches.
image_metadata.description: Three-step pipeline diagram from the InstructGPT paper showing: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human-written prompt-response pairs, (2) reward model training from human preference rankings, and (3) PPO-based reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align GPT-3.

image_metadata.caption: Constitutional AI: self-supervised harmlessness training via written principles. (Anthropic, 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Constitutional AI as an alternative to RLHF, specifically to contrast human-feedback-based alignment with principle-based AI self-supervision.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Constitutional AI training pipeline, showing how a set of written principles (a 'constitution') guides a self-critique and revision loop to produce a harmless AI system without direct human supervision at each step.

image_metadata.caption: The three-step RLHF pipeline: SFT, reward modeling, and PPO optimization. (Stiennon et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on RLHF or instruction-tuning; use as a reference diagram when explaining InstructGPT or comparing alignment techniques.
image_metadata.description: Wide-format diagram depicting the three-step RLHF (Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback) procedure: (1) supervised fine-tuning on demonstrations, (2) training a reward model from human preference comparisons, and (3) optimizing the language model policy using PPO against the reward model.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention mechanism. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining how multi-head attention differs from single-head attention; use to walk through each computational step before presenting the mathematical formulation
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel attention heads, including linear projections, scaled dot-product attention blocks, concatenation, and final linear transformation

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show early when introducing the Transformer architecture as a whole; reference back when discussing individual components like attention or positional encoding to anchor them in the full model
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) with multi-head attention and feed-forward layers, and the decoder stack (right) with masked multi-head attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward layers, plus input/output embeddings and positional encoding

image_metadata.caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap, L=32, d=128. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the need for positional encoding due to permutation invariance of self-attention; use to connect the mathematical formula to its visual pattern before discussing learned alternatives
image_metadata.description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating wave patterns across positions and dimensions with values ranging from -1 (black) to 1 (white)

image_metadata.caption: Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) applied to query/key vectors. (Su et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after covering sinusoidal encoding; show when explaining how RoPE encodes relative positions and why it improves context length extrapolation compared to absolute positional encodings
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) showing how 2D rotation matrices are applied to query and key vectors in pairs of dimensions, with rotation angles proportional to position index i and dimension-specific frequencies

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla Transformer vs Transformer-XL training with segment recurrence. (Dai et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the context segmentation problem of vanilla Transformers; use the side-by-side comparison to motivate why Transformer-XL's recurrence mechanism is needed
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of training in vanilla Transformer vs Transformer-XL with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches hidden states from previous segments and extends the effective attention span across segment boundaries

image_metadata.caption: Compressive Transformer with memory and compressed memory tiers. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after Transformer-XL as a natural extension; use to explain the tradeoff between temporal range and memory cost, and to motivate the compression loss objectives
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory tiers: a standard memory buffer of recent past activations and a compressed memory buffer of older activations, with compression functions reducing older memories to save space while retaining information

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights across memory positions in Compressive Transformer. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Compressive Transformer architecture; use as empirical support that compression preserves useful information and that the model attends to both recent and older compressed memories
image_metadata.description: Bar chart showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) across memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right), demonstrating that compressed memories (older positions) receive comparable attention to recent memories

image_metadata.caption: SPALM combining short-term hidden states with long-term k-NN memory. (Yogatama et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing non-differentiable external memory augmentation; use to contrast with purely recurrent approaches and to explain how retrieval-augmented language models work
image_metadata.description: Diagram of SPALM (Sparse Pointer Attention Language Model) showing how short-term memory (past hidden states) and long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore) are combined via a gating mechanism to produce context-aware token representations

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi adds linear distance-based bias to attention scores per head. (Press et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when comparing positional encoding strategies; use to contrast with RoPE and sinusoidal encoding, emphasizing ALiBi's simplicity and its ability to generalize to longer sequences at inference
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) modifies attention scores by subtracting a linear positional bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions, with different slopes per attention head

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer to illustrate how recurrence replaces fixed-depth stacking; contrast with standard Transformer layer diagram to highlight the iterative refinement concept.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Universal Transformer's recurrent refinement loop: a sequence of token representations is updated repeatedly across multiple time steps in parallel, with each step applying self-attention and a transition function to all positions simultaneously.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer encoder-decoder with shared recurrent weights and cross-attention. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the loop diagram (Image 2) to give the full encoder-decoder picture; useful when comparing Universal Transformer to standard seq2seq Transformers.
image_metadata.description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Soft mask function controls adaptive attention span boundary via learnable parameter z. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the m_z formula to help students visualize what 'soft' means in this context and how z controls the effective span boundary.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z used in adaptive attention span, showing how it maps query-key distance to a [0,1] value with a smooth sigmoid-shaped transition controlled by parameter z, effectively creating a soft boundary for the attention window.

image_metadata.caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-specific, token-specific, and hybrid. (Elabyad et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching adaptive depth or conditional computation; use to compare granularity levels before discussing CALM, and to motivate why token-level exits are more flexible but harder to train.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of three depth-adaptive classifier configurations: (1) sequence-specific — one exit shared by all tokens in a sequence; (2) token-specific — each token has its own exit; (3) a hybrid approach. Diagrams show how classifiers attach to different transformer layers.

image_metadata.caption: 1D vs 2D local attention spans for pixel query q in Image Transformer. (Parmer et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing local/fixed-context attention mechanisms, especially in the context of image generation; use to contrast with full self-attention before discussing efficiency gains.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image generation in Image Transformer. Each shows a query block (black outline) and the corresponding attention span (cyan outline) for a query pixel q, illustrating how context is restricted to a local neighborhood.

image_metadata.caption: Full vs. strided vs. fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing sparse attention mechanisms; use the matrix view to quantify sparsity and the connectivity diagram to explain how information still flows between distant tokens through multi-hop paths.
image_metadata.description: Two-row figure comparing attention connectivity for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention. Top row shows which tokens attend to which in a sequence diagram; bottom row shows the corresponding sparse attention matrices.

image_metadata.caption: Attention patterns compared: ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird global-local combinations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing efficient long-sequence transformer architectures; use as a summary diagram after covering each model individually to reinforce similarities and differences.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side attention pattern diagrams for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird, each showing how global tokens, local sliding-window attention, and random attention are combined. Color-coded blocks indicate different attention types (global, local, random).

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention produces block-diagonal sparse matrix by attending within hash buckets. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (Image 10) to illustrate the resulting attention matrix structure; use to connect the hashing algorithm to the actual computation savings.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of the LSH attention matrix after bucketing and sorting: tokens are grouped into hash buckets (shown by colored blocks along the diagonal), and attention is only computed within each bucket, producing a block-diagonal sparse attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Four steps of LSH attention: bucket, sort, chunk, then attend within chunks. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; walk through each of the four steps sequentially before showing the resulting attention matrix (Image 9).
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram of the four LSH attention stages: (1) bucketing tokens by hash, (2) sorting tokens by bucket assignment, (3) chunking sorted tokens into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention only within each chunk. Uses color coding to track token assignments.

image_metadata.caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear inference time. (Wang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing efficient attention mechanisms, specifically after explaining why standard self-attention is O(L²) and before deriving the Linformer projection math.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) architecture diagram of Linformer showing linear projection layers E and F added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k; (Right) line plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer versus standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.

image_metadata.caption: RFA reorders computation to avoid the full attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how kernel-based methods approximate softmax attention, specifically to illustrate why reordering (φ(Q)(φ(K)ᵀV)) is cheaper than ((QKᵀ)V).
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram contrasting computation order: (Left) standard softmax attention computing the full L×L attention matrix before multiplying by values; (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) reordering operations so the kernel feature map φ is applied first, enabling linear-time computation by avoiding the explicit attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer. (Choromanski et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the Performer's FAVOR+ mechanism, when discussing why orthogonal and positive random features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos alternatives.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel comparison of approximation error plots for the Performer model: (Left) i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features showing orthogonal features have lower approximation error; (Right) sin/cos random features vs. positive random features showing positive features yield lower error for softmax kernel approximation.

image_metadata.caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm for stable RL training. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Transformers for RL, specifically to explain why vanilla Transformer-XL is unstable in RL and how GTrXL's two modifications address this.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) standard Transformer-XL with layer norm after residual addition; (2) Transformer-XL with layer norm reordered to before the sub-layer (pre-norm); (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using pre-norm, designed for stable RL training.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-step prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue example showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly to illustrate the conceptual leap from linear CoT to tree-based deliberate search.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies side by side: Input→Output (standard), Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts — showing how ToT explores a branching tree of intermediate reasoning steps rather than a single linear chain.

image_metadata.caption: PoT generates executable code; CoT reasons in natural language. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why offloading computation to a Python interpreter improves reliability over pure natural language reasoning chains.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to structure tool calls and results. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs are structured to call external tools, especially before discussing Toolformer to establish the general pattern.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating where the LM generates a tool request, where the external API result is inserted, and where the final answer is produced.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively boost task performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the training methodology of TALM to justify why the self-play loop is used rather than a fixed dataset.
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing model performance (e.g., accuracy or F1) across multiple self-play iterations of TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use examples progressively improves the model's performance on downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Toolformer is trained, particularly to illustrate the self-supervised annotation and filtering steps that make it data-efficient.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram illustrating how Toolformer is built: starting from raw text, generating candidate API call annotations via few-shot prompting, executing the API calls, filtering annotations by whether they reduce loss on future tokens, and fine-tuning the LM on the filtered dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls and results. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the Toolformer pipeline diagram to give a concrete data-level example of what the annotation step produces.
image_metadata.description: A text example showing how Toolformer annotates a sentence with API calls inline — e.g., inserting a calculator or search API call with arguments and results directly into the text, illustrating the annotation format used to create training data.

image_metadata.caption: fp16 log-sum-exp errors and bounds: basic vs shifted (Blanchard et al., 2021).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing basic vs shifted log-sum-exp formulas and discussing fp16 overflow thresholds; use it to prompt learners to interpret scatter plots (bound satisfaction, outliers) and to justify why shifting is used in practice.
image_metadata.description: Three scatter plots for fp16 log-sum-exp experiments over many 10D input vectors: (top-left) scaled forward error vs scaled error bound for the basic log-sum-exp (Algorithm 3.1) with a red y=x reference line; (top-right) the same for the shifted log-sum-exp (Algorithm 4.1) using its theoretical bound; (bottom) scaled error of Algorithm 3.1 (x-axis) versus scaled error of Algorithm 4.1 (y-axis), comparing the two implementations point-by-point. Cases where the basic algorithm overflowed are omitted.

image_metadata.caption: Scaled fp16 softmax errors across algorithms (Blanchard et al., 2021).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from log-sum-exp to softmax, especially after explaining that softmax uses exponentials and normalization; use it to have students identify which algorithm clusters closer to the y=x line and discuss why.
image_metadata.description: Scatter plots of fp16 softmax errors (scaled by unit roundoff) for different softmax algorithms, with a red y=x reference line, illustrating how observed errors compare across implementations and against a baseline relationship.

image_metadata.caption: Softmax sum-to-one diagnostic: Algorithm 3.1 vs alternative (Blanchard et al., 2021).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after students implement softmax and observe small inaccuracies; use it as a checklist metric (sum close to 1) to compare implementations and motivate using stable formulations.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the sum of entries of computed softmax vectors for Algorithm 3.1 (red circles) and an alternative softmax algorithm (blue crosses), across many test cases; the x-axis is an arbitrary ordering of test vectors and the y-values show deviation of the softmax sum from the ideal value 1.

image_metadata.caption: Softmax sum-to-one: shifted Algorithm 4.1 vs alternative (Blanchard et al., 2021).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing multiple 'stable softmax' implementations; ask learners to choose an implementation based on how tightly sums cluster around 1 and relate differences to theorems/analysis.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the sum of entries of computed softmax vectors for the shifted softmax algorithm (Algorithm 4.1, red circles) and its alternative version (blue crosses), across many test cases; deviations from 1 indicate loss of normalization accuracy in fp16.

lessons.title: Tokenization & Vocabulary
lessons.content:
# Tokenization & Vocabulary

A language model never “sees” text. It sees a **sequence of integers**. Tokenization is the bridge: it turns a string into **tokens** (IDs), and later turns those IDs back into the original string.

That sounds mundane—until you realize tokenization quietly controls:
- how long your prompts become (cost + latency),
- what the model can represent easily (names, emojis, code, non‑English),
- and a bunch of weird failure modes ("why did it split *that* like *this*?").

## From text → tokens → embeddings
A tokenizer maps text to **`input_ids`** (token IDs). The model then looks up each ID in an **embedding table**: one row per vocabulary item.

In many libraries you’ll also see an **attention mask**: 1s for real tokens, 0s for padding.

```python
from transformers import AutoTokenizer

tok = AutoTokenizer.from_pretrained(
    "distilbert-base-uncased-finetuned-sst-2-english"
)

s = "I love you! I love you!"
print(tok(s)["input_ids"])
print(tok.tokenize(s))
```

The key idea: tokenization defines the model’s “alphabet.” Change the alphabet, and you change what’s easy or hard to say.

## Why subwords? The open-vocabulary problem
If we tokenize by whole words, we need a gigantic vocabulary—and we still lose on rare words (“out-of-vocabulary”). If we tokenize by characters, we can represent anything, but sequences get long.

**Subword tokenization** is the compromise: common chunks become single tokens, rare words become a few pieces. This is why models can handle new names, compounds, and misspellings without needing a special “unknown word” token.

## Byte Pair Encoding (BPE), in one mental model
**BPE** starts with a base vocabulary and repeatedly merges the most frequent adjacent pair.

In **byte-level BPE**, the base vocabulary is the 256 possible bytes (UTF‑8). That’s a superpower: you can represent *any* text, including emojis and arbitrary Unicode, because everything is bytes underneath.

Training looks like:
1. Convert text to bytes.
2. Count adjacent byte-pairs.
3. Merge the most frequent pair into a new token.
4. Repeat until you reach your target **vocabulary size**.

A tiny toy example (conceptually):
```python
from minbpe import BasicTokenizer

t = BasicTokenizer()
text = "aaabdaaabac"

t.train(text, 256 + 3)  # 256 byte tokens + 3 merges
ids = t.encode(text)
print(ids)
print(t.decode(ids))
```

After a few merges, frequent patterns like `"aa"` or `"ab"` become single tokens. Encoding gets shorter, and the model gets to treat those chunks as units.

## Vocabulary size: the knob with real consequences
Choosing **vocab size** is a tradeoff:
- Larger vocab → shorter sequences (faster attention, fewer tokens billed), but bigger embedding/softmax matrices.
- Smaller vocab → longer sequences (slower, more tokens), but smaller matrices and sometimes better compositionality.

There’s no universally best setting; it depends on domain (English vs Japanese vs code) and compute constraints.

## Special tokens (and why they matter)
Most tokenizers reserve IDs for **special tokens** like:
- beginning/end markers (e.g. BOS/EOS),
- padding (PAD),
- separators (SEP),
- “unknown” (UNK) in some schemes.

These aren’t “words”—they’re control symbols that shape how the model segments examples, batches inputs, and knows when to stop.

## A subtle but important detail: pre-splitting rules
Many modern tokenizers apply a regex-like pre-split (letters vs numbers vs punctuation) before BPE merges. This prevents merges across certain boundaries, which can make tokenization more stable and predictable (and avoids some very odd tokens).

## Summary
Tokenization is the model’s interface to text: it defines the discrete symbols the Transformer embeds and predicts. Subword methods like byte-level BPE give open-vocabulary coverage while keeping sequences reasonably short, and choices like vocabulary size, special tokens, and pre-splitting rules directly affect cost, capability, and quirks.

## Recommended Reading
- [minbpe](https://github.com/karpathy/minbpe) — Minimal byte-level BPE implementation you can read end-to-end.
- [Tokenizers: algorithm overview](https://huggingface.co/docs/tokenizers/conceptual/algorithm) — Practical view of tokenizer outputs (`input_ids`, masks) and how tokenizers are used.
- [Neural Machine Translation of Rare Words with Subword Units](https://arxiv.org/abs/1508.07909) — Classic motivation for subwords: open vocabulary via subword units.
- [HF NLP Course: Building a Tokenizer](https://huggingface.co/learn/nlp-course/chapter6/1) — How to train tokenizers and why domain/language matters.
lessons.summary: Tokenization turns text into token IDs that a model can embed and predict, and its design choices shape both efficiency and what the model can express. Byte-level BPE is a common subword approach that achieves open-vocabulary coverage by learning frequent merges over bytes up to a chosen vocabulary size.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention splits inputs across parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the multi-head attention mechanism, especially to contrast single-head vs multi-head attention and explain the concatenation + projection step.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention: input splits into h heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention in parallel, outputs concatenated and linearly projected.

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer overview lesson or when transitioning from individual components to the full model, as a reference map students can return to.
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right), including multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, add & norm, positional encoding, and cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap for L=32, d=128; values range −1 to 1.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the positional encoding formula to give students an intuition for what the encoding looks like and why different positions produce distinguishable patterns.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating sine/cosine patterns across positions and dimensions, with values ranging from -1 (black) to 1 (white).

image_metadata.caption: RoPE encodes position as rotation in 2D subspaces of the embedding. (Su et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching relative positional encodings, especially after sinusoidal encoding, to contrast additive vs rotational approaches and explain RoPE's advantages for long-context tasks.
image_metadata.description: Visual illustration of Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) showing how token embeddings at position i are rotated in 2D subspaces using a rotation matrix, with the rotation angle proportional to position index.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer-XL caches prior segment states to extend attention span. (Dai et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the context segmentation problem in vanilla Transformers and how Transformer-XL's recurrence mechanism solves it.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of training phases for vanilla Transformer (left) and Transformer-XL (right) with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches previous segment hidden states to extend the attention span across segments.

image_metadata.caption: Compressive Transformer uses regular and compressed memory for long context. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Transformer-XL to demonstrate how compressive memory further extends context, and when discussing tradeoffs between memory size and temporal range.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory types: regular memory (recent past activations) and compressed memory (older activations compressed by a compression function), with attention attending to both.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights across memory positions show compressed memory is actively used. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing whether compression loses important information, as empirical evidence that the model attends to compressed memory and it is not wasted.
image_metadata.description: Line plot showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) versus memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right) in the Compressive Transformer, demonstrating that compressed memory receives meaningful attention despite being older.

image_metadata.caption: SPALM combines recurrent short-term and k-NN long-term memory via gating. (Yogatama et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching non-differentiable external memory augmentation, especially to contrast with purely recurrent approaches and explain how retrieval-augmented memory works.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of SPALM architecture showing how short-term memory (past hidden states from Transformer-XL-style cache) and long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore) are combined via a gating mechanism to produce the final output.

image_metadata.caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches scratch-trained Memorizing Transformer performance. (Wu et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing practical deployment of memory-augmented Transformers, particularly to motivate fine-tuning as a cheaper alternative to full retraining.
image_metadata.description: Line chart comparing perplexity vs training steps for: training a memorizing transformer from scratch, fine-tuning a vanilla Transformer with k-NN memory, and a baseline vanilla Transformer, showing fine-tuning achieves comparable performance.

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi adds a negative linear distance bias to attention scores per head. (Press et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching distance-enhanced attention methods, especially to contrast with RoPE and learned positional encodings, and when discussing context length extrapolation strategies.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) showing how a negative linear bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions is added to attention scores before softmax, with different slopes per head.

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi extrapolates best beyond training context length. (Press et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing ALiBi or comparing positional encoding schemes; especially useful when a student asks 'why does positional encoding matter at inference time?' or when discussing out-of-distribution generalization.
image_metadata.description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary/RoPE, T5 relative, and ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve, indicating best extrapolation.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer, particularly when explaining the difference between fixed-depth Transformers and recurrent-depth models, or when discussing parameter sharing across layers.
image_metadata.description: Animated-style diagram showing how Universal Transformer processes a sequence by repeatedly applying the same transformation to all positions in parallel across multiple recurrent steps, with arrows indicating the iterative refinement of hidden states h^t.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer encoder and decoder share recurrent structure; decoder cross-attends to h^T. (Dehghani et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the recurrent step concept (Image 2), when students need to see how Universal Transformer fits into a full seq2seq pipeline or when comparing it to standard Transformer encoder-decoder.
image_metadata.description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Soft mask function smoothly limits attention span via learnable parameter z. (Sukhbaatar et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how adaptive attention span works mechanically, especially when students struggle to connect the formula m_z to its effect on attention weights or when discussing efficient attention mechanisms.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z used in Adaptive Attention Span, showing a piecewise linear sigmoid-like curve that maps distance between query and key to a value in [0,1], with the transition region controlled by learnable parameter z.

image_metadata.caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-specific, token-specific, and mixed. (Elabyad et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing adaptive depth or early-exit models, or when comparing Depth-Adaptive Transformer to CALM; useful when students ask how the model decides how many layers to use.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating three types of depth-adaptive classifiers in Depth-Adaptive Transformer: (1) sequence-specific exit where all tokens share one exit, (2) token-specific exit where each token has its own exit, and (3) a third variant, each shown with exit classifiers attached at multiple layers.

image_metadata.caption: 1D vs 2D local attention spans for image pixels in Image Transformer. (Parmar et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing local/fixed-context attention mechanisms, especially in the context of vision tasks; useful when students ask how Transformer attention is adapted for 2D spatial data.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image pixels in Image Transformer. Each shows a query block (black outline) and the corresponding attention span (cyan outline), illustrating how context is restricted to a local neighborhood in both flattened and 2D grid layouts.

image_metadata.caption: Full, strided, and fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Sparse Transformer or any sparse attention mechanism; ideal when students need to understand how factorized attention patterns approximate full attention at lower cost.
image_metadata.description: Two-row figure comparing attention connectivity patterns and corresponding attention matrices for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention. Top row shows which tokens attend to which; bottom row shows the sparsity pattern in the attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Attention patterns of ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird: local, global, and random.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing global-local attention models, or when students ask how Longformer and Big Bird differ from each other and from ETC; useful as a summary diagram after covering each model individually.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visualization of attention patterns for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird models, each showing a combination of local (sliding window), global (special tokens), and in Big Bird's case, random attention connections overlaid on a sequence.

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention matrix: queries attend only within same hash bucket. (Kitaev et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (complement to Image 10), when students need to see the resulting attention matrix structure or when discussing the computational savings of Reformer.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap-style visualization of the LSH attention matrix showing how queries and keys are grouped into buckets (indicated by colored blocks along the diagonal), with attention restricted to within-bucket pairs, resulting in a block-sparse attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention: four steps — bucketing, sorting, chunking, attention. (Kitaev et al., 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; use before Image 9 to build up from the process to the resulting matrix structure, or when students ask how LSH is implemented in practice.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step process diagram of LSH attention showing four sequential stages: (1) bucketing queries and keys via random projections, (2) sorting by bucket assignment, (3) chunking into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention within chunks.

image_metadata.caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear inference time. (Wang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing efficient attention mechanisms, specifically when explaining how low-rank approximations reduce O(L²) attention to O(Lk) and why this matters for long sequences.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Linformer architecture diagram showing linear projection layers E_i and F_i added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k. (Right) Log-scale plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer vs. standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.

image_metadata.caption: RFA reorders matrix ops to avoid the L×L attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how kernel-based methods approximate softmax attention, particularly to illustrate why reordering operations (right-to-left vs left-to-right) changes computational complexity.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel computation order diagram: (Left) Standard softmax attention showing the expensive O(L²) matrix multiplication order (Q·K^T first, then softmax, then multiply V). (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) showing the reordered computation (φ(K)^T·V first) that enables linear-time attention via kernel approximation.

image_metadata.caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer. (Choromanski et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Performer's FAVOR+ algorithm, specifically to justify why orthogonal and positive random features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos features for approximating softmax attention.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel approximation error comparison for Performer: (Left) Bar/line chart comparing approximation error of i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features across different settings. (Right) Chart comparing approximation error of sin/cos random features vs. positive random features, showing positive features yield lower error.

image_metadata.caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm to stabilize RL training. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Transformers applied to reinforcement learning, specifically to explain why standard Transformer-XL is unstable in RL and how gating and layer norm reordering address this.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) Standard Transformer-XL with layer norm in original position, (2) Transformer-XL with reordered layer norm (pre-norm), and (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using reordered layer norm, enabling stable RL training.

lessons.title: Self-Attention from Scratch
lessons.content:
# Self-Attention from Scratch

A Transformer doesn’t “read left to right” the way an RNN does. Instead, at each layer it builds a new representation for every token by **looking at all tokens** and deciding—dynamically—who matters right now.

That decision is **self-attention**: a learned, input-dependent routing system.

![Self-attention links 'it' to 'animal', visualizing learned word relationships. (Adapted from Alammar)](/api/wiki-images/self-attention/images/jalammar-illustrated-transformer_009.png)

## 1) Attention as a soft lookup

Think of the sequence as a tiny “database” of token information. For each position *t*, we want a context vector:

\[
\text{context}_t = \sum_{j=1}^{T} \alpha_{t,j}\, v_j
\]

The weights \(\alpha_{t,j}\) are **attention weights**: nonnegative, sum to 1, and tell us how much token *t* pulls from token *j*.

So the whole game is: how do we compute \(\alpha\)?

## 2) Why we need Queries, Keys, Values (QKV)

If we used raw embeddings to decide relevance, we’d mostly get “similarity.” But relevance is often *functional*: pronouns should find antecedents; verbs should find subjects; adjectives should find nouns.

So each token gets three learned projections:

- **Query (Q)**: what this token is looking for
- **Key (K)**: what this token offers / how it should be matched
- **Value (V)**: the content to actually copy-mix into the output

In matrix form (sequence length \(T\), model dim \(d\)):

\[
Q = XW^Q,\quad K = XW^K,\quad V = XW^V
\]

## 3) Scaled dot-product attention (the core formula)

For each query token *t*, score every key token *j* with a dot product:

\[
S = QK^\top \quad\Rightarrow\quad S_{t,j} = q_t \cdot k_j
\]

![Step 2: Raw attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Adapted from Alammar)](/api/wiki-images/self-attention/images/jalammar-illustrated-transformer_011.png)

Then we **scale** and **softmax**:

\[
A = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{S}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)
\]

Scaling by \(\sqrt{d_k}\) keeps scores from growing with dimension; otherwise softmax saturates early and gradients get tiny.

![Steps 3–4: Scale scores by √d_k, then apply softmax to normalize. (Adapted from Alammar)](/api/wiki-images/self-attention/images/jalammar-illustrated-transformer_012.png)

Finally, mix values:

\[
\text{Output} = AV
\]

![Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Adapted from Alammar)](/api/wiki-images/self-attention/images/jalammar-illustrated-transformer_015.png)

## 4) Causal masking (for autoregressive language models)

When predicting the next token, position *t* must not peek at future tokens \(j > t\). We enforce this by adding a mask \(M\) to the scores:

- \(M_{t,j} = 0\) if \(j \le t\)
- \(M_{t,j} = -\infty\) if \(j > t\)

Then:

\[
A = \text{softmax}\left(\frac{QK^\top + M}{\sqrt{d_k}}\right)
\]

Softmax turns \(-\infty\) into probability 0, so future positions contribute nothing.

## 5) Minimal NumPy implementation

```python
import numpy as np

def softmax(x, axis=-1):
    x = x - np.max(x, axis=axis, keepdims=True)
    e = np.exp(x)
    return e / np.sum(e, axis=axis, keepdims=True)

def self_attention(X, Wq, Wk, Wv, causal=False):
    # X: (T, d_model)
    Q = X @ Wq  # (T, d_k)
    K = X @ Wk  # (T, d_k)
    V = X @ Wv  # (T, d_v)

    dk = Q.shape[-1]
    scores = (Q @ K.T) / np.sqrt(dk)  # (T, T)

    if causal:
        T = X.shape[0]
        mask = np.triu(np.ones((T, T)), k=1).astype(bool)
        scores = np.where(mask, -1e9, scores)

    A = softmax(scores, axis=-1)      # (T, T)
    out = A @ V                       # (T, d_v)
    return out, A
```

If you print \(A\), you get an **attention map**: row *t* is “where token *t* looks.”

## A note on interpretability

Attention maps are useful for debugging and intuition, but they’re not guaranteed to be faithful explanations of a model’s final decision—information gets remixed across layers and through other components.

## Summary

Self-attention builds each token’s next representation by computing query–key compatibility scores, turning them into attention weights with scaled softmax, and using those weights to mix value vectors. Causal masking is the small but crucial tweak that makes attention safe for next-token prediction.

## Recommended Reading

- [The Illustrated Transformer](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/) — Visual, step-by-step walkthrough of QKV and attention maps.
- [Attention Is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762) — The original Transformer paper introducing scaled dot-product attention.
- [Attention? Attention!](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2018-06-24-attention/) — A deeper tour of attention variants and design choices.
- [Scaled Dot Product Attention](https://jaykmody.com/blog/attention-intuition/) — A clean derivation from “soft lookup” to the final formula.
lessons.summary: Self-attention computes input-dependent connections between all tokens by projecting them into queries, keys, and values, scoring query–key matches with scaled dot products, and mixing values using softmax-normalized attention weights. Causal masking prevents tokens from attending to future positions in autoregressive models.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention links 'it' to 'animal', visualizing learned word relationships. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention conceptually, before the mathematical details, to motivate why attention is useful for capturing word relationships
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization heatmap showing the word 'it' in a sentence attending strongly to 'animal', with colored lines or shading indicating attention weights between the query word and all other words

image_metadata.caption: Steps 3–4: Scale scores by √d_k, then apply softmax to normalize. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the raw scoring diagram (Image 1), when covering steps 3 and 4 of self-attention — use to motivate why we scale before softmax.
image_metadata.description: Diagram extending the scoring steps to include dividing raw scores by √64 (scaling) and applying softmax, producing normalized attention weights that sum to 1, shown alongside value vectors ready to be weighted.

image_metadata.caption: Word-level attention: each word attends differently to others in a sentence.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the visual attention dog example, to transition from image-based intuition to NLP-based attention before introducing formal definitions.
image_metadata.description: A sentence with attention weights visualized as colored arcs or highlights showing how each word attends to other words in the same sentence with varying strengths.

image_metadata.caption: DotProductAttention output: 2×1×4 tensor with valid-length masking applied. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the DotProductAttention formula and before students run the code themselves, to set expectations for what correct output looks like.
image_metadata.description: A code output visualization (likely a heatmap or tensor printout) from running the DotProductAttention class on a toy example with minibatch size 2, 10 keys/values of dimension 4, and valid lengths 2 and 6. Shows the resulting 2×1×4 output tensor or attention weight distribution.

image_metadata.caption: Concrete sentence tokens flowing through encoder self-attention and feed-forward layers. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when walking through a worked example of encoding, after introducing the encoder structure, to connect theory to a concrete sentence
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the encoder processing a short example sentence, showing how each token's vector representation passes through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers with concrete word labels

image_metadata.caption: Step 2: Raw attention scores via dot product of query and key vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing Q/K/V vectors, when explaining step 2 of self-attention — before normalization or softmax are introduced.
