image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram showing how self-attention scores are computed for the word 'Thinking' by taking dot products of its query vector (q1) with the key vectors (k1, k2) of each word in the sequence, producing raw attention scores.

image_metadata.caption: Full self-attention formula: softmax(QK^T/√d_k)V in matrix form. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the W^Q/W^K/W^V matrix diagram (Image 4), as the culminating formula — use to connect the visual steps to the equation seen in the original paper.
image_metadata.description: Single formula diagram showing the complete self-attention computation condensed as: Attention(Q,K,V) = softmax(QK^T / √d_k)V, with the matrix operations visually laid out to show how steps 2–6 collapse into one expression.

image_metadata.caption: All attention heads combined for 'it' — hard to interpret. (Alammar, The Illustrated Transformer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After showing individual attention head visualizations, use this to illustrate the interpretability challenge of multi-head attention and prompt discussion about what each head might specialize in.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to multiple other words, making the combined view cluttered and hard to interpret.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau additive attention: dynamic context vector from all encoder states. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Bahdanau (2015) attention mechanism, after explaining the seq2seq bottleneck, to illustrate the architectural solution.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Bahdanau additive attention encoder-decoder architecture, showing the encoder hidden states connected via attention weights (alpha) to a context vector that feeds into each decoder step, with alignment score computation illustrated.

image_metadata.caption: Self-attention: current word (red) attends to prior context (blue intensity). (Cheng et al., 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing self-attention, to illustrate how a single sequence can attend to itself and why this is useful for capturing dependencies.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of self-attention in a machine reading task: the current word is highlighted in red and blue shading on previous words indicates how strongly each is attended to, showing long-range dependencies within a sentence.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention: parallel heads concatenated and projected. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing the Transformer's attention mechanism; use the left sub-diagram to explain a single head, then the right to explain why multiple heads are used.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) the scaled dot-product attention block with Q, K, V inputs, MatMul, Scale, optional Mask, Softmax, and weighted sum; (right) the multi-head wrapper showing h parallel attention heads whose outputs are concatenated and linearly projected.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention + feed-forward layers with residual connections. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the Transformer encoder architecture, after explaining multi-head attention, to demonstrate how attention is embedded within a larger residual block structure.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding fed into N repeated layers, each containing a multi-head self-attention sub-layer and a position-wise feed-forward sub-layer, both wrapped with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Complete Transformer architecture with encoder-decoder stacks. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately, or when students need to understand the full data flow from input tokens to output probability distribution.
image_metadata.description: Full Transformer architecture diagram combining both encoder (left stack) and decoder (right stack) with all components: embeddings, positional encodings, multi-head attention layers, feed-forward layers, residual connections, layer norm, and the final linear+softmax output, showing data flow between encoder and decoder.

image_metadata.caption: Convolution sees local patches; self-attention accesses all spatial positions globally.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the SAGAN section to motivate why self-attention is added to GANs — students should understand the local vs. global coverage limitation before seeing the SAGAN architecture.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison showing the receptive field of a convolutional operation (small local patch highlighted on a feature map) versus self-attention (entire feature map highlighted), illustrating the difference in spatial coverage between the two operations.

image_metadata.caption: Step 3: Softmax converts alignment scores into attention weights. (Source)

image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating Phase 3 of Bahdanau attention: applying softmax to the alignment score vector to produce attention weights, with each value between 0 and 1 representing the relative importance of each encoder output.

image_metadata.caption: Step 4: Weighted encoder outputs combine into the context vector. (Source)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after softmax step to demonstrate how attention weights are applied to encoder outputs to form the context vector used by the decoder.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing Phase 4 of Bahdanau attention: computing the context vector by element-wise multiplication of attention weights with encoder outputs, illustrating how higher-weighted encoder outputs contribute more to the context vector.

image_metadata.description: A second code output visualization from the DotProductAttention toy example, likely showing attention weight heatmaps for the two minibatch examples with valid lengths 2 and 6, illustrating how masking zeros out weights beyond valid positions.

image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multiple word embedding vectors flowing in parallel through the self-attention and feed-forward sub-layers of an encoder, with each word following its own path

image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the first step of self-attention computation, after motivating why attention is useful, to ground the Q/K/V formalism in concrete matrix operations
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram showing how Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) vectors are derived from input word embeddings by multiplying with learned weight matrices WQ, WK, WV, with vector dimensions labeled (64 vs 512)

image_metadata.caption: Steps 5–6: Weighted value vectors summed to produce self-attention output z1. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the softmax diagram (Image 2), when completing the step-by-step walkthrough of self-attention — ideal for summarizing the full single-head computation before moving to matrix form.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing steps 5 and 6 of self-attention: each value vector is multiplied by its corresponding softmax weight, then all weighted value vectors are summed to produce the final self-attention output vector z1 for the first word.

image_metadata.caption: Matrix form: X multiplied by W^Q, W^K, W^V yields Q, K, V matrices. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after completing the step-by-step word-level walkthrough, when transitioning to the matrix formulation — emphasize the efficiency gain over sequential computation.
image_metadata.description: Matrix-form diagram showing how input embeddings packed into matrix X are multiplied by trained weight matrices W^Q, W^K, and W^V to simultaneously produce the Query (Q), Key (K), and Value (V) matrices for all words in the sequence.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention: eight independent Q/K/V projections run in parallel. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing multi-head attention, after students understand single-head self-attention — use to motivate why multiple heads allow the model to attend to different positions simultaneously.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating multi-head attention by showing the same Q/K/V computation performed eight times in parallel with different weight matrices (W^Q_0…W^Q_7, etc.), producing eight separate sets of Q, K, V matrices and eight Z output matrices.

image_metadata.caption: Eight attention heads produce eight Z matrices — must be combined for feed-forward layer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the multi-head Q/K/V diagram (Image 6), to pose the 'how do we combine eight matrices?' problem before revealing the W^O solution.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the eight Z output matrices (Z0–Z7) produced by eight attention heads, illustrating the challenge that the feed-forward layer expects a single matrix rather than eight separate ones.

image_metadata.caption: Concatenated Z matrices projected by W^O to produce final attention output. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the eight-Z-matrices diagram (Image 7), as the resolution to the dimensionality problem — emphasize that W^O is also trained and learns to combine head outputs meaningfully.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how the eight Z matrices are concatenated into a single wide matrix and then multiplied by the learned weight matrix W^O to produce the final multi-head attention output matrix Z of the correct dimensionality.

image_metadata.caption: Complete multi-head self-attention pipeline from input X to output Z. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a summary after walking through each individual step of multi-head attention — use as a reference diagram students can return to when reviewing the full mechanism.
image_metadata.description: Comprehensive recap diagram showing the entire multi-head self-attention pipeline in one visual: input X, eight sets of W^Q/W^K/W^V weight matrices, eight parallel attention computations, concatenation, W^O projection, and final output Z.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heads show 'it' attending to 'animal' and 'street' — different heads, different focus. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining multi-head attention mechanics, when discussing what attention heads learn in practice — use to motivate why multiple heads improve model capability with a concrete linguistic example.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization showing which tokens the word 'it' attends to across different attention heads when encoding the sentence 'The animal didn't cross the street because it was too tired', with colored lines indicating attention weights to different words.

image_metadata.when_to_show: Use immediately after introducing the concept of positional encoding to give students a tangible, low-dimensional example before scaling up to 512 dimensions.
image_metadata.description: Small concrete numerical example of positional encoding with 4-dimensional embeddings, showing the actual numeric values of positional encoding vectors for the first few positions in a sequence.

image_metadata.caption: 512-dim positional encoding heatmap: each row is one word position. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the 4D example; use this to scale intuition to real model dimensions and discuss why the sinusoidal pattern enables length generalization.

image_metadata.caption: Original paper's interleaved sinusoidal positional encoding pattern. (Vaswani et al., via Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the difference between the paper's positional encoding and the Tensor2Tensor implementation; useful for advanced students reading the original paper.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper showing positional encodings, likely displaying the interleaved sinusoidal pattern (alternating sine/cosine) across dimensions and positions, as referenced in the July 2020 update.

image_metadata.caption: Residual connection and layer normalization around self-attention sub-layer. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining encoder sub-layer architecture; use this to show that self-attention output is not used directly but combined with the original input before normalization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a single encoder layer showing the residual (skip) connection and layer normalization step around the self-attention sub-layer, with vectors flowing in, being processed, added back to the input, and then normalized.

image_metadata.caption: 2-layer Transformer stack with residual connections and layer norm throughout. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining residual connections in a single encoder; use this to show the full stacked architecture and reinforce that the pattern repeats at every sub-layer.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a stacked Transformer with 2 encoders and 2 decoders, showing residual connections and layer normalization around each sub-layer (self-attention and feed-forward) in both encoder and decoder stacks.

image_metadata.caption: Vector-level view: self-attention with residual add and layer normalization. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when students need to trace exactly how a single token's vector is transformed through one encoder sub-layer, especially when debugging understanding of shapes and operations.
image_metadata.description: Detailed vector-level diagram of the self-attention sub-layer with residual connection and layer normalization, showing input vectors x1/x2, the self-attention output z1/z2, the addition of residuals, and the layer-norm output fed to the feed-forward network.

image_metadata.caption: Step 1 of decoding: encoder outputs K/V vectors used by decoder. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the decoder; use this animation to show the first generation step and explain how encoder context is passed to the decoder via K and V vectors.
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF showing the first decoding step of a Transformer: the encoder processes the full input sequence, and the decoder begins generating the first output token, with attention vectors K and V passed from the top encoder to each decoder layer.

image_metadata.caption: Autoregressive decoding: each output token fed back as next decoder input. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the first decoding step animation; use this to show the full generation loop and explain masked self-attention and why future positions must be masked during training.
image_metadata.description: Animated GIF showing multiple decoding steps of a Transformer, illustrating how each generated token is fed back as input to the decoder at the next time step, with the decoder attending to both previous outputs and encoder K/V vectors.

image_metadata.caption: Linear layer produces logits; Softmax converts them to word probabilities. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how the Transformer produces its final word prediction — specifically when transitioning from internal representations to human-readable output tokens.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the final stages of the Transformer decoder: a logits vector of 10,000 cells (one per vocabulary word) produced by the Linear layer, followed by a Softmax layer that converts scores to probabilities, with the highest-probability cell selected as the output word.

image_metadata.caption: One-hot encoding represents 'am' as a single-1 binary vector. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how words are represented as vectors during training, especially before discussing loss functions that compare model output distributions to target one-hot vectors.
image_metadata.description: A one-hot encoding vector example for a small vocabulary, showing how the word 'am' is represented as a vector with a single 1 at the position corresponding to 'am' and 0s elsewhere.

image_metadata.caption: Six-word vocabulary with one-hot target vector for training. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how training targets are formatted — particularly when setting up the loss function discussion and the 'merci' → 'thanks' translation example.
image_metadata.description: A one-hot encoded vector over a six-word vocabulary ('a', 'am', 'i', 'thanks', 'student', '<eos>'), visually showing which position is active for a specific word, used to illustrate target labels in training.

image_metadata.caption: Model output vs. target distribution — the basis of cross-entropy loss. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the loss function — use it to motivate why we need a metric to measure the difference between predicted and target distributions.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of the model's output probability distribution (logits/softmax output across vocabulary) versus the one-hot target label for the word 'thanks', illustrating how loss is computed by comparing the two distributions.

image_metadata.caption: Target probability distributions at each decoding step during training. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining multi-step training targets — after the single-word loss example, to scale up to realistic sentence-level training scenarios.
image_metadata.description: A sequence of probability distributions the model should ideally output at each decoding step for translating 'je suis étudiant' → 'i am a student', showing one distribution per output token with the correct word having near-zero probability in an untrained model.

image_metadata.caption: Trained model outputs near-perfect probability distributions per decoding step. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the untrained/target distributions to contrast before-and-after training, and to introduce decoding strategies like greedy decoding and beam search.
image_metadata.description: Probability distributions produced by a well-trained Transformer model at each decoding step for 'i am a student', showing high probability mass concentrated on the correct output word at each position, contrasting with the untrained model's distributions.

image_metadata.caption: Human visual attention: high-res focus (yellow) with low-res periphery (red).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of introducing attention mechanisms, before any math, to ground the abstract concept in human perceptual experience.
image_metadata.description: Photo of a Shiba Inu dog in a men's outfit, with colored bounding boxes highlighting specific regions: a yellow box on one ear and red boxes on the nose, other ear, and eyes, illustrating selective visual attention.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder-decoder seq2seq: all source info compressed into one context vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitations of vanilla seq2seq models, before introducing attention, so students understand the problem attention solves.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder RNN architecture translating 'she is eating a green apple' to Chinese, with the encoder unrolled left-to-right processing source tokens and the decoder unrolled producing target tokens, connected through a single fixed-length context vector.

image_metadata.caption: Attention alignment matrix: French-to-English translation weights. (Bahdanau et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining alignment scores mathematically, to validate that the learned weights correspond to intuitive word-level translations.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap alignment matrix where rows represent English output words and columns represent French input words; brighter cells indicate higher attention weights, revealing near-diagonal alignment for most words and non-monotonic alignment for reordered phrases.

image_metadata.caption: Soft attention for image captioning: attended regions per generated word. (Xu et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when distinguishing soft vs hard attention, and when demonstrating attention applied to vision tasks beyond NLP.
image_metadata.description: An image of a woman throwing a frisbee in a park, with overlaid attention weight heatmaps showing which image regions the model attends to when generating each word of the caption 'A woman is throwing a frisbee in a park.'

image_metadata.caption: Global vs local attention: full context vs windowed alignment. (Luong et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching Luong et al. (2015) attention variants, after covering Bahdanau attention, to highlight design choices in attention mechanisms.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams comparing global attention (all source hidden states contribute to context) and local attention (only a window of source states around a predicted position contribute), with alignment weight computation shown for each.

image_metadata.caption: Neural Turing Machine: controller + attention-addressed external memory bank.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Neural Turing Machines to illustrate how attention generalizes to memory-augmented neural networks beyond translation tasks.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Neural Turing Machine showing a controller neural network connected to a memory matrix via read and write heads, with attention-based addressing (content-based and location-based) determining which memory rows to access.

image_metadata.caption: Two parameterizations of NTM shift weighting: softmax vs. wrapped Gaussian.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining NTM location-based addressing, specifically when students ask how the controller specifies which memory positions to attend to via convolution-like shifts.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of two methods for representing the shift weighting distribution s_t in Neural Turing Machines: a softmax over discrete shifts and a wrapped Gaussian distribution over shift positions.

image_metadata.caption: NTM addressing pipeline: content → interpolation → shift → sharpening. (Graves et al., 2014)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as a summary diagram after introducing each individual NTM addressing component, or when students need to see how all the pieces (key, beta, g_t, s_t, gamma_t) fit together into one pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A detailed flow diagram showing the full addressing pipeline in a Neural Turing Machine: starting from the controller output, through content-based addressing (key/beta), interpolation gate (g_t), location-based shift convolution (s_t), and sharpening (gamma_t) to produce the final attention weight vector w_t.

image_metadata.caption: Pointer Network: attention weights directly select input positions as outputs. (Vinyals et al., 2015)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Pointer Networks, especially when contrasting with standard NMT attention to highlight that here attention IS the output, not just a context vector.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of a Pointer Network showing an encoder RNN processing input sequence x1…xn and a decoder RNN producing outputs c1…cm, where attention scores over encoder states are used directly as pointers to input positions rather than generating new tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer decoder: masked self-attention, cross-attention, and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the encoder diagram when explaining the full Transformer; emphasize the masking in self-attention and the cross-attention layer that connects encoder and decoder.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer decoder stack showing: output embeddings with positional encoding fed into N repeated layers containing masked multi-head self-attention, encoder-decoder cross-attention, and feed-forward sub-layers, all with residual connections and layer norm, followed by a linear + softmax output layer.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL: interleaved temporal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of the Transformer's weak positional encoding and how SNAIL addresses this for reinforcement learning and meta-learning tasks.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) model showing interleaved temporal convolutional layers (for aggregating context over time) and causal attention layers (for pinpointing specific past information), applied to a sequence of (input, label) pairs for meta-learning.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN self-attention: 1x1 conv branches produce keys, queries, values over spatial features. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the key-query-value framework in Transformers, to demonstrate how the same concept applies to spatial feature maps in image generation models.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the self-attention mechanism in SAGAN showing convolutional feature maps x branched into three 1x1 convolution paths (f→key, g→query, h→value), with attention map beta computed via softmax over dot products, then used to weight value features and produce the attended output o added back via a learned scalar to the input.

image_metadata.caption: SAGAN-generated 128×128 images showing self-attention's role in coherent synthesis. (Zhang et al., 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the SAGAN attention formulation (attention map α_ij, 1x1 conv filters W_f/W_g/W_h, and the residual scale output) to motivate why the added complexity matters — use it as a 'results payoff' moment.
image_metadata.description: A grid of 128×128 images generated by Self-Attention GAN (SAGAN) across multiple ImageNet classes, showing high-quality synthesized images of animals, objects, and scenes with fine structural details.

image_metadata.caption: Attention pooling: query-key compatibility scores weight value aggregation. (D2L.ai Fig. 11.1.1)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show this diagram immediately when introducing the QKV attention framework, before or alongside the softmax weight formula, to anchor the mathematical notation to a visual structure.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the attention mechanism: a query q is compared against multiple keys k_i using a compatibility function a(q,k), the resulting scores are passed through softmax to produce attention weights, and these weights are used to compute a weighted linear combination of values v_i to produce the output.

image_metadata.caption: Heatmap of attention weights across queries and keys. (D2L.ai, Ch. 11.1.1)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the show_heatmaps function and before discussing specific attention patterns, to help students understand how to read and interpret attention weight heatmaps.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap visualization output from the show_heatmaps function, displaying attention weight matrices as color-coded grids where intensity represents the magnitude of attention weights assigned to different key positions for given queries.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heatmap showing selective key weighting for given queries. (D2L.ai, Ch. 11.1.1)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how attention weights can represent selective focus, particularly when contrasting uniform vs. peaked attention distributions.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap visualization showing a specific attention weight pattern — likely a diagonal or identity-like pattern — produced by the show_heatmaps function, demonstrating how a model can learn to attend to specific key positions.

image_metadata.caption: Comparative attention heatmap illustrating varied query-key weight patterns. (D2L.ai, Ch. 11.1.1)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside other heatmap examples when comparing the effect of different queries or attention functions, to build intuition through contrast.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap visualization output from show_heatmaps demonstrating another attention weight pattern, possibly showing a different query set or a different scoring function, to contrast with previous examples.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weight heatmap demonstrating a distinct attention distribution pattern. (D2L.ai, Ch. 11.1.1)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when summarizing the range of possible attention behaviors, or when discussing degenerate cases like uniform attention to contrast with focused attention.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap visualization from show_heatmaps showing yet another attention weight configuration, likely demonstrating a specific property such as uniform attention, sharp attention, or the effect of a particular scoring function.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder-decoder RNN: encoder reads source, decoder emits target words. (Source)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of the NMT lesson, before introducing attention, to establish the baseline encoder-decoder bottleneck problem.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of an encoder-decoder RNN architecture for neural machine translation, showing the encoder reading source words up to an <eos> token and the decoder emitting target words one at a time.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau alignment model: each target word attends to a unique context vector.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from the fixed-context encoder-decoder to Bahdanau attention, to formalize the key mathematical difference.
image_metadata.description: Mathematical formula showing the conditional probability of each target word yi conditioned on a distinct context vector ci, along with the alignment model eij that scores how well input position j matches output position i using hidden state si-1 and annotation hj.

image_metadata.caption: Full Bahdanau attention pipeline: bidirectional encoder feeds selective decoder. (Source)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as an overview before diving into each individual step of the Bahdanau attention process, so students have a mental map of the full pipeline.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step procedural diagram of the full Bahdanau attention process, illustrating how a bidirectional RNN encoder produces annotations and how the decoder uses attention over those annotations to generate each target word.

image_metadata.caption: Step 1: Collect encoder outputs to compute alignment scores. (Source)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when beginning the step-by-step walkthrough of the Bahdanau decoder, specifically at the 'collecting encoder outputs' phase.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating Phase 1 of the attention decoder: collecting all encoder hidden state outputs and pairing them with the current decoder hidden state to compute alignment scores at each time step.

image_metadata.caption: Step 2: Linear layers + tanh combine hidden state and encoder outputs. (Source)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show during the explanation of how alignment scores are computed, specifically to illustrate the role of linear projections and tanh in the scoring network.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing Phase 2 of Bahdanau attention: the decoder hidden state and encoder outputs each pass through separate Linear layers with trainable weights, are combined, and then passed through a tanh activation to produce intermediate alignment scores.

image_metadata.caption: Step 5: Context vector + decoder RNN cell produces next word prediction. (Source)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the final step of the Bahdanau attention walkthrough to complete the end-to-end picture of how one decoding time step produces an output word.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating Phase 5 of Bahdanau attention: the context vector is concatenated with the decoder output, fed into the decoder RNN cell to produce a new hidden state, and then passed through a linear classifier to predict the next word.

image_metadata.caption: Luong multiplicative attention: matrix multiplication replaces additive scoring. (Source)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from Bahdanau to Luong attention, to highlight the architectural simplification and efficiency gain of multiplicative scoring.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the full decoding output step and transitioning to Luong (multiplicative) attention, illustrating how simple matrix multiplications between encoder and decoder states produce attention scores more efficiently than Bahdanau's additive approach.

image_metadata.caption: Global attention: decoder hidden state ht compared to all encoder states hs. (Paperspace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the global attention variant of Bahdanau attention, specifically after explaining the encoder-decoder bottleneck problem and before discussing the score function used for alignment.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a global attention mechanism showing how the decoder hidden state ht is compared to all encoder hidden states hs to produce a variable-length alignment vector at, which is then used to compute the context vector ct for the current decoding time step.

image_metadata.caption: Attention pooling: scoring function + softmax yields weighted value average. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on attention mechanisms, before introducing specific scoring functions, to establish the overall computational structure students will be filling in.
image_metadata.description: A flow diagram (Fig. 11.3.1) showing how attention pooling computes a weighted average of values. It illustrates the pipeline: queries and keys feed into an attention scoring function 'a', the scores pass through softmax to produce attention weights, which then weight the values to produce the output.

image_metadata.caption: AdditiveAttention weight heatmap with valid-length masking applied. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show side-by-side with the DotProductAttention heatmap to help students compare the two scoring functions and understand when each is preferred.
image_metadata.description: A second output visualization from the AdditiveAttention example, likely an attention weight heatmap showing the distribution over 10 keys for both minibatch examples, with masking applied at positions 2 and 6 respectively.

image_metadata.caption: AdditiveAttention intermediate output showing learned projection effect. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when explaining the parameter matrices W_q and W_k to show how they project queries and keys to the same dimensionality h before scoring.
image_metadata.description: A third output visualization from the AdditiveAttention example, likely showing intermediate computations or the final 2×1×4 output tensor, demonstrating the role of the learnable weight matrices W_q, W_k, and w_v in the scoring function.

image_metadata.caption: Final AdditiveAttention output: 2×1×4 tensor matching DotProductAttention shape. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the final step of the AdditiveAttention walkthrough, then prompt students to compare outputs with DotProductAttention to consolidate understanding of both methods.
image_metadata.description: A fourth and final output visualization from the AdditiveAttention toy example, likely the final attended output tensor of shape 2×1×4, completing the demonstration of the additive attention mechanism.

image_metadata.caption: Context vector (size 4) passed from encoder to decoder — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing seq2seq models and explaining how information is compressed into a fixed-size vector; use before discussing attention to motivate why the bottleneck is a problem
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the context vector in a seq2seq encoder-decoder architecture, visualizing a size-4 hidden state vector passed from encoder to decoder in machine translation

image_metadata.caption: Word embeddings feed into RNN time steps, producing hidden states — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining how words are fed into an RNN encoder; show before the context vector diagram to build up from word → embedding → hidden state → context
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating word embeddings and RNN mechanics, showing how words are converted to vectors and how hidden states are passed between RNN time steps during encoding

image_metadata.caption: Attention: encoder passes all hidden states to decoder — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: When transitioning from classic seq2seq to attention models; show immediately after explaining the context vector bottleneck to illustrate the proposed solution
image_metadata.description: High-level diagram contrasting attention-based seq2seq with classic seq2seq, showing the encoder passing all hidden states (not just the last) to the decoder

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla seq2seq: encoder compresses source into fixed context vector. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on attention mechanisms to establish the baseline seq2seq model and highlight its limitation of compressing all source information into a single fixed vector.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of a standard seq2seq encoder-decoder architecture showing how a variable-length source sequence is compressed into a fixed-dimensional context vector by the RNN encoder, which is then used by the RNN decoder to generate the target sequence token by token.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau attention: decoder dynamically attends to relevant encoder states. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the motivation for attention, when explaining how the decoder query attends to encoder keys/values at each decoding step.
image_metadata.description: Detailed architectural diagram of the Bahdanau attention-based seq2seq model, showing how the decoder at each time step computes attention weights over all encoder hidden states to produce a dynamic context vector, which is then used alongside the decoder hidden state to generate the next token.

image_metadata.caption: Training loss curve for Seq2SeqAttentionDecoder implementation. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when walking through the training procedure of the Bahdanau attention model to confirm the model is learning and to discuss convergence behavior.
image_metadata.description: Training loss curve (loss vs. epoch or iteration) for the Seq2SeqAttentionDecoder model during initial training, showing the model's learning progress over time.

image_metadata.caption: Training loss for Bahdanau attention decoder (alternate framework). (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside other training curves when comparing implementations across frameworks or when discussing the effect of hyperparameters on training.
image_metadata.description: Training loss curve for the Bahdanau attention seq2seq model, likely showing a different training run or framework variant (e.g., MXNet, JAX, or TensorFlow) compared to image 3.

image_metadata.caption: Training loss for Bahdanau attention model, third framework variant. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing training results across multiple frameworks to reinforce that the Bahdanau attention mechanism behaves consistently.
image_metadata.description: Training loss curve for the Bahdanau attention seq2seq model, representing another framework or configuration variant.

image_metadata.caption: Training loss for Bahdanau attention model, fourth framework variant. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as part of a complete set of training curves when discussing cross-framework reproducibility of the Bahdanau attention implementation.
image_metadata.description: Training loss curve for the Bahdanau attention seq2seq model, representing a fourth framework or configuration variant.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heatmap: learned alignment between English source and French target. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after training is complete when explaining how attention weights can be interpreted as alignment scores, and when discussing the interpretability advantage of attention over fixed context vectors.
image_metadata.description: Attention weight heatmap visualizing which encoder hidden states (source tokens) the decoder attends to at each decoding time step when translating an English sentence to French, showing the alignment pattern learned by the Bahdanau attention mechanism.

image_metadata.caption: Attention alignment heatmap for second English-French translation example. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the first attention heatmap to compare alignment patterns across different translation examples and reinforce understanding of dynamic attention.
image_metadata.description: Attention weight heatmap for a second English-to-French translation example, showing the soft alignment pattern produced by the trained Bahdanau attention model.

image_metadata.caption: Attention heatmap for third translation example showing selective focus. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as part of a series of attention heatmaps when teaching students to interpret attention patterns and discuss cases where alignment is clear versus diffuse.
image_metadata.description: Attention weight heatmap for a third English-to-French translation example, displaying the decoder's attention distribution over encoder states at each generation step.

image_metadata.caption: Attention alignment heatmap for fourth English-French translation example. (D2L.ai)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show as the final example in a series of attention heatmaps to consolidate understanding of attention-based alignment before moving to Transformer architectures.
image_metadata.description: Attention weight heatmap for a fourth English-to-French translation example, showing the final trained model's attention alignment pattern.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention: project Q/K/V into h heads, attend, concat, project (D2L).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks why multiple heads help, or when transitioning from single scaled dot-product attention to the multi-head formulation; also use it while tracing shapes/operations during implementation (project per head → attend → concat → final linear layer).
image_metadata.description: Block diagram of multi-head attention: input queries (Q), keys (K), and values (V) are each linearly projected into h separate heads (different learned projection matrices per head), each head performs attention pooling in parallel, the h head outputs are concatenated, then passed through a final linear projection to produce the multi-head attention output.

image_metadata.caption: Attention over words: dot-product scores → weights → weighted value average (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the attention equation to map each symbol (Q,K,V, scores, weights) to a visual step; revisit when explaining why keys/values are per-token and how changing the query changes which tokens are emphasized.
image_metadata.description: A word-sequence attention illustration: one query vector is compared via dot products to each word’s key vector to produce attention weights (softmax implied), which are then used to compute a weighted average of the corresponding value vectors, yielding a context/output vector.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention: parallel heads, concatenate, project (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after students understand single-head attention; use it to explain what changes in code (reshape/split into heads, parallel attention, concat) and to motivate heads attending to different relations.
image_metadata.description: Multi-head attention schematic: Q, K, V are linearly projected into multiple heads, each head runs scaled dot-product attention independently, head outputs are concatenated, then passed through a final linear projection.

image_metadata.caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding across positions and dimensions (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right after presenting the PE(pos,i) formula to connect the equation to an interpretable signal; use it when explaining why adjacent dimensions form phase-shifted pairs.
image_metadata.description: Plot of sinusoidal positional encoding values across positions for selected hidden dimensions, illustrating sine on even indices and cosine on odd indices with different wavelengths per dimension.

image_metadata.caption: Heatmap of sinusoidal positional encodings (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when students struggle to interpret PE beyond the formula; also useful when discussing how maximum sequence length and embedding size affect positional resolution.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap visualization of positional encodings over sequence position (x-axis) and hidden dimension (y-axis), showing repeating wave patterns with increasing wavelengths for higher dimensions and phase shifts between neighboring dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer learning-rate warm-up and decay schedule (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing training instabilities or reproducing Transformer baselines; use it to guide selection of warm-up steps and to explain why constant LR often fails early.
image_metadata.description: Learning-rate schedule plot for Transformer training with warm-up: learning rate increases linearly during early steps (warm-up) and then decays afterward according to a schedule (commonly inverse square-root).

image_metadata.caption: Attention maps by layer and head for a seq2seq task (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after training when evaluating whether the model learned the task; use it to teach how to read attention matrices (query position as row, key position as column) and compare heads/layers.
image_metadata.description: Grid of attention maps for a sequence-to-sequence task: rows correspond to Transformer layers and columns to attention heads; each subplot is a SeqLen×SeqLen matrix showing where each token attends (row-wise softmax).

image_metadata.caption: CIFAR-100 set with one anomalous image among same-class images (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing the dataset sampling procedure and model; use it to confirm students understand the task definition (identify the odd-one-out) and the need for order-agnostic processing.
image_metadata.description: Example set for anomaly detection built from CIFAR-100 images: a small collection of images where most belong to the same class and one image is from a different class (the anomaly).

image_metadata.caption: Attention maps reveal heads focusing on the outlier image (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: After students understand self-attention computation, show this when explaining how to debug/interpret a set-based Transformer: ask them to identify which heads focus on the suspected outlier (last index) and relate that to the model’s anomaly prediction.
image_metadata.description: Grid of multi-head self-attention heatmaps for a Transformer applied to set anomaly detection, showing attention probabilities between each pair of images in a 10-image set (9 similar tree images and 1 out-of-distribution volcano). Each subplot corresponds to a specific layer and head, with axes representing query image index attending to key image index; several heads concentrate attention on the last image, indicating it is treated as salient/anomalous.

image_metadata.caption: Layer-wise head behaviors: outlier-focused vs averaging attention (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing how different layers contribute differently to a decision: have learners compare Layer 2/3 vs Layer 4 patterns and hypothesize which heads compute a set summary versus which heads highlight anomalies.
image_metadata.description: Second grid of Transformer attention heatmaps across layers/heads for the same set anomaly detection example. It highlights contrasting behaviors: some mid-layer heads place high attention on the last (anomalous) image, while later-layer heads (Layer 4) assign it very low attention, and some heads (e.g., Layer 3 Head 2–4) distribute attention more evenly, resembling a weighted average over the set.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder–decoder with attention and feed-forward sublayers (Vaswani et al.).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Transformer at a high level, or when a learner confuses encoder self-attention with decoder masked self-attention and cross-attention; also useful before walking through tensor shapes per sublayer.
image_metadata.description: Full Transformer encoder–decoder architecture diagram showing stacked encoder and decoder blocks with multi-head self-attention, encoder–decoder attention, position-wise feed-forward networks, residual connections + layer normalization, and input/output embeddings with positional encodings leading to a linear + softmax output.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention: project, attend per head, concatenate, project (Vaswani et al.).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner needs more visual clarity on how heads are formed and recombined, especially during implementation discussions (e.g., reshaping tensors into [batch, heads, seq, d_k]).
image_metadata.description: A larger rendering of Figure 2 emphasizing the multi-head attention pipeline: separate linear projections create per-head Q/K/V, each head computes scaled dot-product attention, head outputs are concatenated, then passed through an output projection.

image_metadata.caption: Encoder self-attention links “making” to distant dependency (Vaswani et al.).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining self-attention to illustrate how it links distant words; use when comparing Transformers to RNNs on handling long-range dependencies.
image_metadata.description: Attention visualization for the encoder self-attention (layer 5 of 6) focusing on the token “making”; colored lines/weights from “making” to other tokens show multiple heads attending strongly to a distant phrase completion “making … more difficult.”

image_metadata.caption: Attention heads focusing “its” on antecedents (Vaswani et al.).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing interpretability of attention heads or giving examples of emergent linguistic structure in Transformers; useful in lessons on coreference signals.
image_metadata.description: Anaphora-related attention visualization: top shows full attention patterns for a specific head (head 5) in encoder layer 5/6; bottom isolates attention originating from the word “its” for heads 5 and 6, showing very sharp, focused attention to likely antecedents.

image_metadata.caption: Isolated “its” attentions across heads show coreference-like links (Vaswani et al.).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a learner asks why multiple heads are needed or how to interpret per-head attention maps; pair with a discussion of limitations of attention-as-explanation.
image_metadata.description: Second panel associated with Figure 4: additional attention visualization emphasizing isolated attentions from the token “its” across two heads (5 and 6), highlighting concentrated links to specific earlier tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Example head showing sentence-structure-related attention (Vaswani et al.).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching that heads can learn different tasks (syntax vs coreference vs locality); use as a prompt for students to hypothesize what a head is tracking.
image_metadata.description: Sentence-structure-related attention visualization example 1 from encoder self-attention layer 5/6: a single head’s attention pattern aligns tokens in a way that appears to reflect syntactic structure (e.g., grouping related words/phrases).

image_metadata.caption: A second head with different structure-focused attention behavior (Vaswani et al.).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after image 7 to compare two heads; use when explaining that heads are not redundant and can encode different relational inductive biases.
image_metadata.description: Sentence-structure-related attention visualization example 2 from a different encoder self-attention head (layer 5/6), contrasting with the previous example to show distinct learned attention behaviors across heads.

image_metadata.caption: Luong score functions: dot, general (W_a), concat (tanh + v_a). (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student asks how dot/general/concat differ, or before deriving attention weights (softmax over scores) so they can identify where parameters enter and why dot product requires matching hidden sizes.
image_metadata.description: Three side-by-side computation graphs comparing Luong attention score functions: (1) dot product scoring between decoder state h_t and encoder state h_s; (2) general/bilinear scoring using a learned matrix W_a between h_t and h_s; (3) concat scoring that concatenates h_t and h_s, applies a learned layer W_a with tanh, then projects with v_a^T to a scalar alignment score.

image_metadata.caption: FLOPs vs sequence length for Luong score functions. (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing performance trade-offs or selecting an attention mechanism for long sequences; use it to justify choosing dot product when speed/throughput is a constraint.
image_metadata.description: Log-scale line plot of FLOPs versus source sequence length S comparing dot product, general (bilinear), and concat attention scoring; dot product grows most slowly and remains lowest, general is higher due to an extra matrix multiply, and concat is highest due to additional layer and projection.

image_metadata.caption: Parameter counts for dot, general, and concat attention vs hidden size. (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student is tuning hidden size or comparing architectures under a parameter budget; pair with the FLOPs plot to separate compute cost from parameter cost.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing parameter counts across hidden dimensions d for dot product (no parameters), general attention (one d×d matrix W_a), and concat attention (matrix W_a plus vector v_a), showing parameter growth with d and the relative overhead of each method.

image_metadata.caption: Global attention heatmap: broad support with diagonal alignment. (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing attention weights/softmax to illustrate typical alignment patterns; use it when contrasting global vs local attention and discussing O(S) per-step cost.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap of global attention weights for 10 target time steps over a 12-token source sequence, with a strong diagonal band indicating roughly monotonic alignment while still assigning non-zero probability mass across most source positions.

image_metadata.caption: Local attention heatmap with window D=2 around alignment point. (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when a student asks how local attention works or why it is faster; use it to explain monotonic/local-m assumptions and the effect of changing D on coverage.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap of local attention weights over the same target/source lengths, where non-zero weights appear only in a narrow diagonal window around a predicted alignment point p_t, corresponding to a window half-width D=2.

image_metadata.caption: Bahdanau: compute context from h_{t-1} before RNN update. (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing Bahdanau vs Luong or when a student is confused about whether attention uses h_{t-1} or h_t; use it to trace the computation order step-by-step.
image_metadata.description: Flow diagram of Bahdanau attention placement: context vector is computed using the previous decoder hidden state h_{t-1} before the RNN update, then the context is concatenated with the current input and fed into the RNN to produce h_t.

image_metadata.caption: Luong: compute attention after RNN, combine into \tilde{h}_t for output. (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when implementing Luong attention or debugging tensor flow; use it to explain the order: RNN → scores/weights → context → combine → logits.
image_metadata.description: Flow diagram of Luong attention placement: the decoder RNN runs first to produce the current hidden state h_t, then attention scores and the context vector are computed from h_t and encoder states, and finally a learned combination produces the attentional state \tilde{h}_t for output prediction.

image_metadata.caption: Dot-product attention: peaked weights across source positions. (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when validating an implementation (check masking and sum-to-one) or when comparing how different score functions change distribution sharpness after training.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart of dot-product attention weights over 10 source positions for one example, showing a peaked distribution with near-zero weights on some positions (including padded/masked positions).

image_metadata.caption: General attention: W_a reshapes alignment weights vs dot product. (Brenndörfer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show right after the dot-product bar chart to contrast parameter-free vs learned scoring; use it when explaining why general attention can work with differing representation spaces.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart of general (bilinear) attention weights over the same 10 source positions, illustrating a distribution shaped by the learned matrix W_a and differing from the dot-product pattern.

image_metadata.caption: Concat attention weights with padding masked (Marc Brenndörfer).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when debugging or explaining why attention must ignore padding; ask the learner to check that padded bars are ~0 and remaining bars sum to 1.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart of concat (additive) attention weights over source token positions for one decoder step, with two rightmost positions shaded gray to indicate padding; non-padded weights sum to ~1 and padded positions receive near-zero weight due to masking.

image_metadata.caption: Simulated translation alignment heatmap (Marc Brenndörfer).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use after introducing attention weights to illustrate alignment patterns; prompt learners to identify diagonal vs. reordered alignments and relate them to source/target word order.
image_metadata.description: Attention heatmap for a simulated English-to-French translation: rows correspond to generated French target words and columns to English source words; strongest weights form an approximately diagonal band with a notable off-diagonal shift where French adjective-noun order differs (e.g., 'noir' attending to 'black').

image_metadata.caption: Context vector as weighted encoder-state sum (Marc Brenndörfer).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use immediately after presenting attention weights to show how they produce the context vector; have learners compute one dimension manually from the displayed contributions.
concepts: weighted sum (convex combination) of encoder states
image_metadata.description: Stacked bar chart across context-vector dimensions showing each dimension as a weighted sum of encoder-state components; contributions are color-coded by source word with 'cat' (red) dominating, and diamond markers indicating the final context vector values matching the stacked totals.

image_metadata.caption: Context vector resembles 'cat' but blends all tokens (Marc Brenndörfer).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after computing the context vector to interpret its meaning; ask learners which encoder state it most resembles and why it is not identical.
image_metadata.description: Grouped bar chart comparing each encoder state’s feature values (including the 'cat' state in red) against the resulting context vector; the context vector closely matches the 'cat' encoding but is slightly shifted toward 'The' and 'sat' due to their smaller weights.

image_metadata.caption: Larger score scales make softmax attention nearly one-hot (Marc Brenndörfer).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing why unscaled dot-product attention can be problematic at high dimensions; have learners relate sharper distributions to poorer gradient flow.
image_metadata.description: Grouped bar charts showing softmax attention weights for increasing score scales (e.g., 1 to 50): as scale increases, probability mass concentrates almost entirely on the highest-scoring position, illustrating increasingly sharp/peaked attention.

lessons.title: Multi-Head Attention & Positional Encoding
lessons.content:
# Multi-Head Attention & Positional Encoding

Self-attention is already a superpower: each token can “look at” other tokens and pull in the information it needs. But a single attention operation is still just one way of looking. **Multi-head attention** is the Transformer’s way of saying: “Let’s look in several different ways at once.”

## From one attention to many heads

In scaled dot-product attention, we build **queries (Q)**, **keys (K)**, and **values (V)** from the same input (in self-attention). Each token’s query scores every token’s key, we softmax those scores into weights, then take a weighted sum of values.

Multi-head attention repeats this in parallel:

- We linearly project the model’s representation into *h* smaller subspaces (the **heads**).
- Each head runs attention independently.
- We concatenate all head outputs and project back to the model dimension.

Why bother? Because different subspaces can specialize. One head might focus on local syntax (nearby tokens), another on long-range coreference (“it” referring to something earlier), another on delimiter/formatting cues, etc. You don’t hard-code these roles—the model discovers them because multiple heads make it easier to represent multiple relationship types simultaneously.

![Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads and projection (adapted from Vaswani et al., via Weng).](/api/wiki-images/multi-head-attention/images/lilianweng-posts-2018-06-24-attention_013.png)

## A tiny shape-check (PyTorch-ish)

```python
# x: (B, T, d_model)
q = x @ Wq  # (B, T, d_model)
k = x @ Wk
v = x @ Wv

# split into heads
q = q.view(B, T, h, d_head).transpose(1, 2)  # (B, h, T, d_head)
k = k.view(B, T, h, d_head).transpose(1, 2)
v = v.view(B, T, h, d_head).transpose(1, 2)

scores = (q @ k.transpose(-2, -1)) / (d_head ** 0.5)  # (B, h, T, T)
att = scores.softmax(dim=-1)
out = att @ v  # (B, h, T, d_head)

# merge heads
out = out.transpose(1, 2).contiguous().view(B, T, d_model)
out = out @ Wo
```

The key idea: heads don’t add new *operations*—they add parallel *representation capacity*.

## The missing ingredient: order

Attention alone is permutation-invariant: if you shuffle tokens, the same set of dot products exists, just reordered. So Transformers inject position information using **positional encodings**.

### Sinusoidal positional encoding (classic)

The original Transformer adds a deterministic vector to each token embedding based on its position. Each dimension is a sine or cosine at a different frequency, so positions have a smooth, structured pattern.

![Actual positional encoding values for a 4D embedding (adapted from Alammar).](/api/wiki-images/multi-head-attention/images/jalammar-illustrated-transformer_023.png)

This “add-to-embeddings” trick is simple and works well, but modern LLMs often prefer *relative* position methods.

## Relative position methods: RoPE and ALiBi

Two popular upgrades:

- **RoPE (Rotary Positional Embeddings)**: instead of adding a position vector to embeddings, RoPE *rotates* the Q and K vectors in a position-dependent way (in 2D pairs of dimensions). The dot product between rotated Q and K naturally encodes **relative offsets** (how far apart tokens are), which is exactly what attention cares about.

- **ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases)**: rather than modifying embeddings, ALiBi adds a head-specific linear penalty to attention scores based on distance. Farther tokens get a larger negative bias (with different slopes per head), encouraging a controllable “recency preference” while still allowing long-range attention when needed.

A useful mental model: sinusoidal encoding tells each token “you are at absolute position *t*.” RoPE/ALiBi make it easier for attention to reason in terms of “this token is *k* steps away from me.”

## Summary

**Multi-head attention** runs several attention mechanisms in parallel so the model can represent different kinds of relationships at the same time. **Positional encodings** supply order information; classic sinusoidal encodings add absolute position signals, while **RoPE** and **ALiBi** bake relative distance more directly into attention.

## Recommended Reading

- [The Illustrated Transformer](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-transformer/) — Clear, visual walkthrough of multi-head attention and positional encodings.
- [Tutorial 6: Transformers and Multi-Head Attention](https://uvadlc-notebooks.readthedocs.io/en/latest/tutorial_notebooks/tutorial6/Transformers_and_MHAttention.html) — Implementation-oriented explanation with practical details.
- [Attention? Attention!](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2018-06-24-attention/) — Deeper technical treatment of attention variants and Transformer components.
- [Attention Is All You Need](https://arxiv.org/abs/1706.03762) — The original Transformer paper.
lessons.summary: Multi-head attention improves self-attention by running several attention “views” in parallel, letting different heads specialize in different relationships. Positional encodings (sinusoidal, RoPE, ALiBi) inject token order—often by making attention sensitive to relative distances between tokens.

image_metadata.caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: As a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately; use for review or to answer questions about how the full model fits together.
image_metadata.description: Complete Transformer model architecture diagram showing the full encoder stack on the left and decoder stack on the right, with the cross-attention connection between them, input/output embeddings, positional encodings, and the final linear+softmax output layer.

image_metadata.caption: Actual positional encoding values for a 4-dimensional embedding. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after introducing the concept of positional encoding; use as a stepping stone before showing the full 512-dimensional heatmap
image_metadata.description: Small color-coded grid showing actual positional encoding values for a 4-dimensional embedding across several word positions, with values ranging from -1 to 1 displayed as colored cells

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding from 'Attention Is All You Need' paper (interleaved). (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the difference between the paper's positional encoding method and the Tensor2Tensor implementation; use to teach students to read primary sources alongside blog explanations
concepts: Attention Is All You Need paper
image_metadata.description: Reproduction of the positional encoding visualization from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, showing the interleaved sinusoidal pattern across positions and dimensions as a color-coded heatmap

image_metadata.caption: Full Transformer architecture: encoder-decoder with multi-head attention. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use as a capstone diagram after explaining encoder and decoder separately, or as an overview at the start of a Transformer lesson to orient students.
image_metadata.description: Complete Transformer architecture diagram showing the full encoder (left) and decoder (right) stacks side by side, with data flow from input/output embeddings through all layers to the final softmax output probabilities.

image_metadata.caption: All attention heads combined for 'it' — hard to interpret. (Alammar, The Illustrated Transformer)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After showing individual attention head visualizations, use this to illustrate the complexity of multi-head attention and prompt discussion about how different heads specialize.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to various other words, making the combined picture cluttered and hard to interpret.

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding pattern for 4-dimensional embeddings. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Immediately after explaining that positional encodings are added to embeddings; use as a stepping stone before the large 512-dimension heatmap.
image_metadata.description: Small heatmap visualization of positional encoding values for a short sequence with 4-dimensional embeddings. Each row represents a word position and each column a dimension, color-coded to show values between -1 and 1.

image_metadata.caption: Original paper's interleaved sine/cosine positional encoding heatmap. (Vaswani et al., via Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing the original paper's positional encoding formula; use alongside the Tensor2Tensor version to highlight that multiple valid implementations exist.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap of positional encodings from the original 'Attention Is All You Need' paper, showing the interleaved sine/cosine encoding pattern across positions and dimensions, slightly different from the Tensor2Tensor implementation.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads and projection. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer architecture; introduce single-head attention first, then use this diagram to show how multi-head attention generalizes it.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, scaling, optional masking, softmax, and weighted sum; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, concatenation, and linear projection.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: positional encoding, self-attention, and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the Transformer encoder in detail; use to walk through each component layer by layer before discussing the decoder.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing a multi-head self-attention sublayer and a position-wise feed-forward sublayer, both with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: All attention heads for 'it' — multi-head attention visualized. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining individual attention heads; use to motivate why we need multiple heads and to discuss the interpretability trade-off of richer representations
image_metadata.description: Visualization of all attention heads simultaneously for the word 'it' in a sentence, showing overlapping colored lines connecting 'it' to various other words, creating a complex, hard-to-interpret web of attention weights

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (4D example). (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; use the 4D example to build intuition before showing the full 512-dimensional version
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing positional encoding vectors being added to word embedding vectors before they enter the encoder, with a small 4-dimensional example illustrating the sinusoidal pattern of positional encoding values

image_metadata.caption: 512-dimensional positional encoding heatmap across word positions. (Alammar, 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the 4D example; use to show the full-scale pattern and discuss the property of generalizing to longer sequences than seen during training
image_metadata.description: Large color-coded heatmap grid where each row represents the positional encoding vector for one word position (up to ~20 positions) and each column represents one of 512 dimensions, with colors ranging from dark to light showing sinusoidal patterns across dimensions

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention with parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the Transformer's core attention mechanism; show left panel first for single-head attention, then right panel to explain multi-head extension.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of multi-head scaled dot-product attention showing: (left) single scaled dot-product attention with Q, K, V inputs, softmax, and scaling; (right) multi-head attention with h parallel attention heads, linear projections, concatenation, and final linear layer.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer encoder: self-attention + feed-forward layers with residuals. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining the Transformer encoder architecture; use to walk through each sub-layer and the role of residual connections and normalization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Transformer encoder stack showing: input embeddings with positional encoding, followed by N repeated layers each containing multi-head self-attention and a position-wise feed-forward network, both with residual connections and layer normalization.

image_metadata.caption: SNAIL: interleaved temporal convolutions and attention for meta-learning. (Mishra et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When discussing limitations of the Transformer for sequential/RL tasks and how SNAIL addresses them by combining convolution and attention.
image_metadata.description: SNAIL (Simple Neural AttentIve Learner) architecture diagram showing alternating temporal convolutional layers (for dense context aggregation) and attention layers (for selective retrieval), used for meta-learning tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Positional encoding vectors added to word embeddings (4D example). (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing positional encoding; use this before showing the full 512-dimensional heatmap to build intuition with a simple 4D example.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing positional encoding vectors being added to word embedding vectors before they enter the encoder. Each word's embedding is shown as a colored vector, with a corresponding positional encoding vector added to it, illustrated with a 4-dimensional example.

image_metadata.caption: 512-dimensional positional encoding heatmap — sinusoidal patterns visible. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After the 4D example; use to show the full-scale pattern and discuss how the sinusoidal structure allows generalization to unseen sequence lengths.
image_metadata.description: Large heatmap showing positional encodings for many sequence positions (rows) across 512 dimensions (columns). Color-coded from -1 to 1, revealing sinusoidal wave patterns that vary in frequency across dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head attention: parallel heads then concat and project (UvA DLC).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after students understand single-head attention, to motivate multiple heads and to explain tensor shapes (split into heads, then concat).
image_metadata.description: Multi-head attention schematic: Q, K, V are linearly projected into multiple heads (separate subspaces), each head runs scaled dot-product attention independently, the head outputs are concatenated, and a final linear projection produces the layer output.

image_metadata.caption: CAM vs. GETAM: transformers capture full object shapes, not just discriminative regions.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson on weakly-supervised segmentation to motivate why transformer-based attention maps are preferable to CNN CAMs.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of CNN-based CAM activation maps versus GETAM (transformer-based) activation maps on multiple object images, showing that CAM highlights only discriminative regions while GETAM captures full object shapes.

image_metadata.caption: Naive CAM transplantation to ViT fails; a new approach is required.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why a new method is needed for ViT-based segmentation, after introducing the CAM concept and before presenting GETAM.
image_metadata.description: Comparison of three CAM variants applied to ViT: CAM (add) which adds the [class] token to patch tokens, CAM (ignore) which ignores the [class] token, and CNN baseline CAM (ResNet38), showing that naive transplantation of CNN CAM to ViT produces poor results.

image_metadata.caption: Class-agnostic attention (left) vs. class-wise gradient maps (right) in GETAM.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the two components of GETAM (attention map and gradient map) to illustrate what each component contributes before explaining their combination.
image_metadata.description: Grid of visualizations showing class-agnostic attention maps (leftmost column) and class-wise gradient maps (rightmost column) for multiple images, demonstrating that gradient maps retain clear object-specific shapes while attention maps show broader candidate regions.

image_metadata.caption: GETAM combines class-agnostic attention and gradient maps inside each transformer block.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the GETAM formulation in detail, as the primary architectural reference diagram for the method.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of GETAM generation within a single transformer block, showing how the class-agnostic attention map (A_CLS^i) and the class-wise gradient map (∇A_CLS) are computed and combined via element-wise multiplication to produce the GETAM output.

image_metadata.caption: Successive transformer blocks focus on different object regions, enabling better coverage.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the successive attention aggregation component of GETAM, to justify why multiple transformer blocks are combined rather than using a single layer.
image_metadata.description: Grid of attention maps from successive transformer blocks for two sample images (one with multiple people), showing how different layers focus on different object regions (foreground vs. background people) without low-level noise.

image_metadata.caption: Element-wise multiplication concentrates attention; matrix multiplication introduces noise.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show during the ablation study discussion or when justifying the element-wise multiplication design choice in GETAM.
