```json
{
  "label": "positive",
  "confidence": 0.78,
  "rationale": "..."
}
```

In practice, you’ll combine: system prompt (rules) + few-shot examples (format imitation) + explicit schema (contract). This reduces “creative formatting” and makes failures easier to detect.

## 6) In-context learning: why any of this works
All of the above are forms of **in-context learning**: the model uses the prompt as short-term working memory. You’re not updating weights; you’re shaping behavior by providing instructions, patterns, and constraints inside the context window.

![Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Yao et al.; via Weng, 2023)](/api/wiki-images/chain-of-thought/images/lilianweng-posts-2023-03-15-prompt-engineering_002.png)

*CoT is a single reasoning path; more advanced variants explore multiple paths and then pick a best answer.*

## Summary
Prompting is a control surface: with the right instructions, examples, and output constraints, you can unlock much stronger performance without changing the model. The most common “stack” for reliability is system prompt + few-shot demonstrations + structured output, with chain-of-thought for tasks that require multi-step reasoning.

## Recommended Reading
- [Prompt Engineering Guide — Techniques](https://www.promptingguide.ai/techniques) — A practical catalog of prompting patterns (zero-shot, few-shot, CoT, and more).
- [Chain-of-Thought Prompting](https://www.promptingguide.ai/techniques/cot) — Clear examples of CoT and zero-shot CoT.
- [Prompt Engineering (Lilian Weng)](https://lilianweng.github.io/posts/2023-03-15-prompt-engineering/) — Deep, well-structured overview of in-context prompting and reasoning techniques.
- [Techniques to Improve Reliability](https://cookbook.openai.com/articles/techniques_to_improve_reliability) — Why models fail on complex tasks and how prompting can fix it.
lessons.summary: Prompting techniques—zero-shot, few-shot, chain-of-thought, system prompts, and structured output—can dramatically change LLM performance without any weight updates. Treat prompts as a spec: set rules, show examples, and enforce an output contract to make behavior more reliable.
lessons.video_title: Chain-of-Thought Prompting Elicits Reasoning in Large Language Models (Paper Explained)

image_metadata.caption: Number of examples needed: prompting vs. finetuning decision boundary. (Chip Huyen)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When students ask 'how much data do I need to finetune?' — use this to anchor the answer and transition into discussing prompt tuning as a middle ground
image_metadata.description: Chart showing the relationship between number of training examples and the recommended approach (prompting vs. finetuning), illustrating that prompting works with few examples while finetuning requires hundreds or more

image_metadata.caption: Human memory taxonomy mapped to LLM agent memory components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the memory module to establish the human memory taxonomy before mapping each type to its LLM agent equivalent.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical diagram categorizing human memory into sensory memory (iconic, echoic, haptic), short-term/working memory, and long-term memory (explicit/declarative and implicit/procedural), with mappings to LLM agent memory equivalents.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-pass prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly after covering CoT and self-consistency, to illustrate the conceptual leap to tree-based exploration.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies side by side: Input→Output (standard), Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts — showing how ToT explores a branching tree of intermediate reasoning steps with evaluation and backtracking.

image_metadata.caption: PoT generates executable code instead of verbal reasoning steps. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing programming-language-based prompting methods (PAL/PoT), especially to contrast with CoT and motivate why symbolic execution is more reliable for math.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to structure tool calls and results. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs interface with external tools at the token level, before or alongside Toolformer to compare approaches.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating where the LLM generates a tool request, where the external API result is inserted, and where the final answer is produced.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve model performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the TALM self-play mechanism to provide quantitative justification for why iterative tool-use training works.
image_metadata.description: A performance graph showing model accuracy across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use training examples progressively improves the model's performance on downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching Toolformer as a case study in self-supervised tool augmentation, after introducing TALM to highlight the shared self-play philosophy and differences in implementation.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram of Toolformer's training process: starting from raw text, generating candidate API call annotations via in-context learning, executing the API calls, filtering annotations by whether they reduce loss on future tokens, and fine-tuning the LM on the filtered dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for calculator, search, and calendar. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the Toolformer pipeline diagram to ground the abstract training steps in a concrete annotated example, especially when discussing how different tool types are handled uniformly.
image_metadata.description: A concrete text example showing how Toolformer annotates a sentence with API calls — embedding calculator, Wikipedia search, and calendar tool calls inline within natural text, with results inserted, demonstrating the annotation format used to create training data.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-step prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue example showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly to illustrate the conceptual leap from linear CoT to tree-based deliberate search.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies side by side: Input→Output (standard), Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts — showing how ToT explores a branching tree of intermediate reasoning steps rather than a single linear chain.

image_metadata.caption: PoT generates executable code; CoT reasons in natural language. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why offloading computation to a Python interpreter improves reliability over pure natural language reasoning chains.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to structure tool calls and results. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs are structured to call external tools, especially before discussing Toolformer to establish the general pattern.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating where the LM generates a tool request, where the external API result is inserted, and where the final answer is produced.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively boost task performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the training methodology of TALM to justify why the self-play loop is used rather than a fixed dataset.
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing model performance (e.g., accuracy or F1) across multiple self-play iterations of TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use examples progressively improves the model's performance on downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how Toolformer is trained, particularly to illustrate the self-supervised annotation and filtering steps that make it data-efficient.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram illustrating how Toolformer is built: starting from raw text, generating candidate API call annotations via few-shot prompting, executing the API calls, filtering annotations by whether they reduce loss on future tokens, and fine-tuning the LM on the filtered dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls and results. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the Toolformer pipeline diagram to give a concrete data-level example of what the annotation step produces.
image_metadata.description: A text example showing how Toolformer annotates a sentence with API calls inline — e.g., inserting a calculator or search API call with arguments and results directly into the text, illustrating the annotation format used to create training data.

image_metadata.caption: fp16 log-sum-exp errors and bounds: basic vs shifted (Blanchard et al., 2021).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing basic vs shifted log-sum-exp formulas and discussing fp16 overflow thresholds; use it to prompt learners to interpret scatter plots (bound satisfaction, outliers) and to justify why shifting is used in practice.
image_metadata.description: Three scatter plots for fp16 log-sum-exp experiments over many 10D input vectors: (top-left) scaled forward error vs scaled error bound for the basic log-sum-exp (Algorithm 3.1) with a red y=x reference line; (top-right) the same for the shifted log-sum-exp (Algorithm 4.1) using its theoretical bound; (bottom) scaled error of Algorithm 3.1 (x-axis) versus scaled error of Algorithm 4.1 (y-axis), comparing the two implementations point-by-point. Cases where the basic algorithm overflowed are omitted.

image_metadata.caption: Scaled fp16 softmax errors across algorithms (Blanchard et al., 2021).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from log-sum-exp to softmax, especially after explaining that softmax uses exponentials and normalization; use it to have students identify which algorithm clusters closer to the y=x line and discuss why.
image_metadata.description: Scatter plots of fp16 softmax errors (scaled by unit roundoff) for different softmax algorithms, with a red y=x reference line, illustrating how observed errors compare across implementations and against a baseline relationship.

image_metadata.caption: Softmax sum-to-one diagnostic: Algorithm 3.1 vs alternative (Blanchard et al., 2021).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after students implement softmax and observe small inaccuracies; use it as a checklist metric (sum close to 1) to compare implementations and motivate using stable formulations.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the sum of entries of computed softmax vectors for Algorithm 3.1 (red circles) and an alternative softmax algorithm (blue crosses), across many test cases; the x-axis is an arbitrary ordering of test vectors and the y-values show deviation of the softmax sum from the ideal value 1.

image_metadata.caption: Softmax sum-to-one: shifted Algorithm 4.1 vs alternative (Blanchard et al., 2021).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing multiple 'stable softmax' implementations; ask learners to choose an implementation based on how tightly sums cluster around 1 and relate differences to theorems/analysis.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the sum of entries of computed softmax vectors for the shifted softmax algorithm (Algorithm 4.1, red circles) and its alternative version (blue crosses), across many test cases; deviations from 1 indicate loss of normalization accuracy in fp16.

image_metadata.caption: Same prompt, different outputs: LLMs produce inconsistent results by default. (Chip Huyen)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing why LLMs are difficult to deploy reliably in production; use before discussing temperature=0 as a mitigation strategy
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating how the same LLM prompt can produce different outputs across multiple runs, showing the stochastic nature of LLM responses and how silent failures occur when prompts are accidentally modified

image_metadata.caption: Temperature=0 enforces consistency but doesn't guarantee trustworthy LLM outputs. (Chip Huyen)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining temperature=0 as a fix for stochasticity; use to prompt discussion about what 'reliable' really means beyond just determinism
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the analogy of a teacher giving consistent scores only in one specific room — visualizing how setting temperature=0 achieves consistency but doesn't fully solve trust or reliability issues in LLM outputs

image_metadata.caption: Prompting vs. finetuning: trade-offs in cost, latency, and flexibility. (Chip Huyen)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing the prompting vs. finetuning decision; use as a decision-making framework before diving into data requirements for each approach
image_metadata.description: Comparison diagram contrasting prompting versus finetuning approaches for adapting LLMs, likely showing trade-offs in cost, latency, flexibility, and model size across the two paradigms

image_metadata.caption: Prompt tuning optimizes prompt embeddings, bridging prompting and finetuning. (Chip Huyen)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After covering prompting and finetuning; introduce as a bridge technique when discussing parameter-efficient adaptation methods
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating prompt tuning: instead of modifying the text of a prompt, the embedding of the prompt is programmatically optimized, showing performance comparison with prompt engineering and full model tuning on T5

image_metadata.caption: Stanford Alpaca: distilling text-davinci-003 into LLaMA-7B for under $600. (Chip Huyen)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching distillation or cost-efficient LLM deployment; use to show students that frontier model capabilities can be approximated cheaply
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating the Alpaca distillation pipeline: 52k instructions fed into text-davinci-003 (175B parameters) to generate outputs, which are then used to finetune LLaMA-7B, producing a small model that mimics a large one

image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing LLM agent architectures; use to show students how traditional programming constructs map onto prompt-driven orchestration
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing four LLM agent control flow patterns: sequential (tasks in order), parallel (tasks simultaneously), if-statement (conditional branching), and for-loop (iterative execution), with LLM-determined conditions

image_metadata.caption: LLM pipeline decomposes a natural language query into structured search steps. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after presenting the keyword-search limitation (Image 1) to illustrate how an LLM-powered pipeline solves the problem of intent-aware search.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing a multi-step LLM pipeline for intelligent search: breaking down a natural language query (e.g., 'things you need for camping in Oregon in November') into structured steps — likely including query understanding, item generation, and product retrieval — to return a curated, context-aware product list.

image_metadata.caption: Extended LLM search pipeline for context-aware product recommendations. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside or immediately after Image 2 to provide a complementary or extended view of the LLM search pipeline, useful when discussing pipeline variants or additional steps like ranking and filtering.
image_metadata.description: A follow-up or alternative diagram of the LLM-based search pipeline, likely showing a refined or extended version of the steps for converting a contextual user query into product recommendations, possibly including additional stages such as ranking, filtering, or LLM-generated item lists fed into a product database.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Press et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing retrieval-augmented or decomposition-based prompting strategies, especially to contrast with standard single-pass prompting.
image_metadata.description: A step-by-step dialogue showing the Self-Ask prompting method, where the LLM decomposes a complex question into follow-up sub-questions, issues external search queries for each, retrieves answers, and synthesizes a final answer.

image_metadata.caption: Tree of Thoughts explores branching reasoning paths vs. linear CoT. (Yao et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching advanced reasoning prompting methods, particularly after covering CoT and self-consistency, to illustrate the conceptual leap to tree-based exploration.
image_metadata.description: A diagram comparing four reasoning strategies side by side: Input→Output (standard), Chain-of-Thought, Self-Consistency with CoT, and Tree of Thoughts — showing how ToT explores a branching tree of intermediate reasoning steps with evaluation and backtracking.

image_metadata.caption: PoT generates executable code instead of verbal reasoning steps. (Chen et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing programming-language-based prompting methods (PAL/PoT), especially to contrast with CoT and motivate why symbolic execution is more reliable for math.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of Chain-of-Thought (CoT) and Program of Thoughts (PoT) prompting on a math word problem, showing CoT producing natural language reasoning steps while PoT generates Python code that is executed by an interpreter to compute the answer.

image_metadata.caption: TALM uses special tokens to structure tool calls and results. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how LLMs interface with external tools at the token level, before or alongside Toolformer to compare approaches.
image_metadata.description: A formatted text example showing the TALM API call structure, with special tokens |tool-call, tool input, |result, and |output demarcating where the LLM generates a tool request, where the external API result is inserted, and where the final answer is produced.

image_metadata.caption: TALM self-play iterations progressively improve model performance. (Parisi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the TALM self-play mechanism to provide quantitative justification for why iterative tool-use training works.
image_metadata.description: A performance graph showing model accuracy across multiple self-play iterations in TALM, demonstrating that iterative bootstrapping of tool-use training examples progressively improves the model's performance on downstream tasks.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer pipeline: annotate, execute, filter, and fine-tune on API calls. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching Toolformer as a case study in self-supervised tool augmentation, after introducing TALM to highlight the shared self-play philosophy and differences in implementation.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram of Toolformer's training process: starting from raw text, generating candidate API call annotations via in-context learning, executing the API calls, filtering annotations by whether they reduce loss on future tokens, and fine-tuning the LM on the filtered dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Toolformer annotates text with inline API calls for calculator, search, and calendar. (Schick et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the Toolformer pipeline diagram to ground the abstract training steps in a concrete annotated example, especially when discussing how different tool types are handled uniformly.
image_metadata.description: A concrete text example showing how Toolformer annotates a sentence with API calls — embedding calculator, Wikipedia search, and calendar tool calls inline within natural text, with results inserted, demonstrating the annotation format used to create training data.

image_metadata.caption: LLM agent architecture: planning, memory, and tool use components. (Weng, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on LLM agents to establish the overall system structure before explaining each subsystem in detail.
image_metadata.description: A high-level architecture diagram of an LLM-powered autonomous agent system, showing the LLM as the central brain connected to components: Planning (with sub-components like reflection and decomposition), Memory (sensory, short-term, long-term), and Tool Use (external APIs, databases, etc.).

image_metadata.caption: ReAct reasoning trajectories across knowledge and decision-making tasks. (Yao et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the ReAct framework, specifically to contrast the Thought-Action-Observation loop against an Act-only approach and explain why reasoning traces improve performance.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of ReAct reasoning trajectories for knowledge-intensive tasks (HotpotQA, FEVER) and decision-making tasks (AlfWorld, WebShop), showing interleaved Thought, Action, and Observation steps versus Act-only baselines.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion's self-reflection loop: actor, evaluator, and memory buffer. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how agents can self-correct, particularly after introducing the concept of trial-and-error in autonomous agents and before discussing the heuristic function details.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the Reflexion framework, showing the cycle between Actor (LLM), Evaluator (reward/heuristic), Self-Reflection module, and Memory buffer, with arrows indicating how failed trajectories feed into self-reflection and updated memory for the next trial.

image_metadata.caption: Reflexion outperforms baselines; hallucination dominates over inefficient planning. (Shinn & Labash, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the Reflexion framework to validate its claims with data, and use the hallucination vs. inefficient planning breakdown to deepen discussion of agent failure modes.
image_metadata.description: Bar charts comparing Reflexion against baselines on AlfWorld and HotpotQA benchmarks, broken down by failure type (hallucination vs. inefficient planning), showing performance across multiple trials.

image_metadata.caption: CoH fine-tuning: model learns incremental improvement from ranked feedback sequences. (Liu et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how LLMs can be trained to improve outputs using feedback sequences, especially when contrasting CoH with RLHF or other feedback-based training methods.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing how Chain of Hindsight (CoH) fine-tuning works: a sequence of model outputs annotated with feedback labels (Bad, Good, Better, Best) is fed as input, and the model learns to produce progressively improved outputs conditioned on that sequence.

image_metadata.caption: Algorithm Distillation: distilling RL learning histories into a neural network. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing in-context RL and Algorithm Distillation, particularly to explain how learning histories encode an algorithm rather than just task-specific knowledge.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating Algorithm Distillation (AD): source policies generate multi-episode learning histories for various tasks, which are then used to train a neural network via behavioral cloning to produce a task-agnostic in-context RL policy.

image_metadata.caption: AD matches or exceeds RL² on memory and exploration tasks. (Laskin et al., 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining AD to validate its performance claims and to discuss why multi-episode context (2–4 episodes) is necessary for near-optimal in-context RL to emerge.
image_metadata.description: Performance comparison charts of AD, ED (Expert Distillation), source policy, and RL² across memory and exploration environments (dark environments using A3C, watermaze using DQN), measuring cumulative reward over in-context episodes with binary reward.

image_metadata.caption: MIPS algorithm comparison: recall@10 vs. query speed tradeoffs. (Google Blog, 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing external memory retrieval in agents, specifically to explain why ANN is preferred over exact search and how different algorithms balance speed vs. accuracy.
image_metadata.description: A comparison chart of Maximum Inner Product Search (MIPS) / Approximate Nearest Neighbor (ANN) algorithms (e.g., HNSW, FAISS, ScaNN) measured by recall@10 versus query speed, showing the accuracy-speed tradeoff across methods.

image_metadata.caption: HuggingGPT's four-stage pipeline: LLM plans, selects, and coordinates AI models. (Shen et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the 'Tool Use' component of LLM agents, specifically to demonstrate how a real system (HuggingGPT) chains task planning → model selection → execution → response, reinforcing the four-stage pipeline described in the text.
image_metadata.description: A multi-stage pipeline diagram showing HuggingGPT's architecture: user request flows through task planning, model selection, task execution, and response generation stages, with the LLM acting as a central controller coordinating specialized AI models from HuggingFace.

image_metadata.caption: API-Bank pseudo-code showing LLM decision steps for tool selection and execution. (Li et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing HuggingGPT to deepen understanding of how tool-use accuracy is measured; use it when discussing how researchers evaluate agent capability at granular decision levels rather than just final output quality.
image_metadata.description: Pseudo-code block illustrating the decision logic an LLM follows when making API calls in the API-Bank benchmark, showing conditional branching for API selection, parameter filling, and call execution with evaluation checkpoints at each step.

image_metadata.caption: Generative agent architecture: memory stream, retrieval, reflection, and planning loop. (Park et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when transitioning from tool-use agents to socially-interactive agent simulations; use it to contrast reactive tool-use architectures with agents that require persistent memory and self-reflection to sustain coherent long-term behavior.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the Generative Agents system showing how a single LLM-powered agent processes observations through a memory stream, retrieves relevant memories via a retrieval module, reflects to form higher-level insights, and plans actions — all feeding back into behavior in a sandbox simulation environment.

lessons.title: RAG & Retrieval-Augmented Generation
lessons.content:
# RAG & Retrieval-Augmented Generation

LLMs are incredible at *sounding* right. That’s also the problem: when they don’t know something, they’ll often still produce a fluent answer—sometimes confidently wrong. **Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG)** is the practical fix: instead of forcing the model to “remember” everything, we let it *look things up* in your documents at question time.

## The core idea: “generate, but with receipts”

A RAG system has two brains working together:

1. **Retriever**: finds relevant snippets from a document collection.
2. **Generator (LLM)**: writes the final answer *conditioned on those snippets*.

This helps with:
- **knowledge cutoffs** (the model is stuck in the past)
- **domain depth** (your niche docs weren’t in training)
- **private/proprietary data** (your internal wiki is not on the internet)
- **trust** (you can show the retrieved passages as evidence)

## Embeddings: turning meaning into geometry

To retrieve by meaning (not just keyword match), we use **embeddings**: a model maps text → a vector (a list of numbers). Similar meanings land near each other in this vector space.

So we embed:
- each document chunk → a vector
- the user query → a vector

Then we search for the closest chunk vectors to the query vector.

## Vector databases and cosine similarity

A **vector database** stores these chunk vectors and supports fast “nearest neighbor” search.

A common similarity measure is **cosine similarity**:
- 1.0 means vectors point in the same direction (very similar)
- 0 means unrelated
- -1 means opposite

In practice, RAG often retrieves the **top-k** most similar chunks.

## Chunking: the make-or-break preprocessing step

We can’t embed (or stuff into a prompt) entire books. So we **chunk** documents into smaller pieces.

Good chunking is a Goldilocks problem:
- too small → loses context (“this sentence refers to *what*?”)
- too big → retrieval gets fuzzy and prompts get expensive

A common baseline: split by headings/paragraphs, target a few hundred tokens per chunk, and optionally add overlap.

## Putting it together: the RAG pipeline

Here’s the minimal mental model:

```python
# 1) Index time
chunks = chunk_documents(docs)
chunk_vecs = embed(chunks)
vector_db.upsert(vectors=chunk_vecs, payloads={"text": chunks})

# 2) Query time
q_vec = embed([user_question])[0]
hits = vector_db.query(vector=q_vec, top_k=5)
context = "\n\n".join([h.payload["text"] for h in hits])

prompt = f"""
Answer the question using ONLY the context.
If the context is insufficient, say you don't know.

Context:
{context}

Question: {user_question}
"""
answer = llm(prompt)
```

That one pattern—**retrieve → inject context → generate**—is the heart of RAG.

## RAG vs. “just fine-tune it”

| Approach | Best for | Weaknesses |
|---|---|---|
| **RAG** | factual Q&A over changing/private docs; citations; fast updates | depends heavily on chunking + retrieval quality; context window limits |
| Fine-tuning | style, format, behavior (“talk like our brand”) | expensive; slow to update; not ideal for precise factual recall |

*Table informed by common RAG vs fine-tuning guidance across production RAG writeups.*

## A useful extension: decomposing questions before retrieval

Sometimes the best retrieval query isn’t the user’s original question. You can have the model break a question into sub-questions and retrieve iteratively.

![Self-Ask: LLM decomposes questions and queries external search. (Adapted from Lilian Weng)](/api/wiki-images/rag-retrieval/images/lilianweng-posts-2023-03-15-prompt-engineering_001.png)

This is the same spirit as RAG: don’t guess—*fetch the missing info*.

## Summary

**RAG** reduces hallucination by grounding an LLM’s response in retrieved document chunks. The quality of a RAG system is often dominated by unglamorous details—**chunking**, **embeddings**, and **retrieval similarity**—because those determine what “evidence” the model gets to see.

## Recommended Reading
- [Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG)](https://www.pinecone.io/learn/retrieval-augmented-generation/) — Clear explanation of why LLMs hallucinate and how retrieval grounding helps.
- [A comprehensive guide for building RAG-based LLM applications (Part 1)](https://www.anyscale.com/blog/a-comprehensive-guide-for-building-rag-based-llm-applications-part-1) — End-to-end RAG pipeline details (chunk, embed, index, serve, evaluate).
- [The Illustrated Word2vec](https://jalammar.github.io/illustrated-word2vec/) — Intuition for embeddings as geometry (the foundation of vector retrieval).
- [Retrieval-Augmented Generation for Knowledge-Intensive NLP Tasks](https://arxiv.org/abs/2005.11401) — The classic RAG formulation combining parametric and non-parametric memory.
lessons.summary: Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) grounds an LLM’s answers by retrieving relevant document chunks from a vector database and injecting them into the prompt. This reduces hallucinations and enables up-to-date, domain-specific, and private knowledge without retraining the model.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF uses human feedback as a reward signal to optimize language models. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of an RLHF lesson when introducing why standard loss functions and metrics like BLEU/ROUGE are insufficient for capturing human preferences in language generation.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the high-level concept of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), showing how human feedback is used as a reward signal to optimize a language model beyond simple next-token prediction loss.

image_metadata.caption: Pretraining produces the base LM used to generate reward model training data. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the RLHF pipeline stages, specifically before discussing reward model training, to ground students in the prerequisite step of having a pretrained base language model.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the language model pretraining stage in the RLHF pipeline, showing how a base language model is trained before being fine-tuned with human feedback, serving as the starting point for reward model data generation.

image_metadata.caption: A reward model assigns scalar scores to text, encoding human preferences for RL training. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining pretraining and before introducing the RL fine-tuning stage, when students need to understand how human preferences are operationalized as a numeric reward for policy optimization.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the reward model training stage in the RLHF pipeline, illustrating how a preference/reward model is trained to assign scalar scores to text outputs, with context on model size choices used by OpenAI, Anthropic, and DeepMind.

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization

image_metadata.caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context

image_metadata.caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens

image_metadata.caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years

image_metadata.caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
image_metadata.description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields

image_metadata.caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases

image_metadata.caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with calculus/ML background who benefit from seeing loss landscape shapes
image_metadata.description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's labeler UI: rank responses 1–7 and by preference to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic and what it implies about subjectivity in human feedback
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of the actual web UI used by OpenAI labelers to rank InstructGPT responses, showing a prompt, multiple model-generated responses, a 1–7 rating scale, and a ranking interface

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: SFT → Reward Model → RL fine-tuning. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an overview, and revisit when explaining the RL fine-tuning phase to anchor the KL divergence constraint in the broader pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), (2) Reward Model training, and (3) RL fine-tuning with the reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT model. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the conceptual motivation for the KL constraint; use to transition students from intuition to formal understanding before discussing the InstructGPT paper's notation.
image_metadata.description: A mathematical equation displaying the RLHF objective function, showing it as the sum of two terms: the expected reward from the reward model and a KL divergence penalty term that constrains the RL-tuned policy from straying too far from the SFT model.

image_metadata.caption: Schulman's proposed RL-based solutions to LLM hallucination. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the hallucination section, before presenting the InstructGPT empirical results, to create a productive tension between Schulman's hypothesis and the actual findings.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs know what they know and that RLHF could in principle be used to force models to only output information they are confident about.

image_metadata.caption: Two complementary strategies for high-quality human annotation data. (Weng 2024)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on human data quality to orient students before diving into specific methods; use as a running reference to locate each new technique within the overall framework.
image_metadata.description: A two-branch diagram showing two complementary approaches to achieving high data quality: one branch focused on improving rater quality (e.g., better annotators, training, guidelines) and another on aggregation/modeling techniques (e.g., majority vote, weighted aggregation, learned models).

image_metadata.caption: Quality assurance as a feedback loop over annotation quality attributes. (Daniel et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing how to operationalize data quality standards; useful before explaining specific QA mechanisms like inter-annotator agreement or rater audits.
image_metadata.description: A structured diagram illustrating the quality assurance process for crowdsourced annotation, showing how quality attributes feed into a quality model, which in turn drives quality assurance actions that loop back to improve the annotation process.

image_metadata.caption: Non-expert AMT workers match experts in MT evaluation when spammers are down-weighted. (Callison-Burch 2009)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the wisdom-of-the-crowd concept and when discussing how to handle low-quality annotators; pairs well with explaining weighting schemes for aggregating noisy labels.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Bar chart comparing expert-expert vs. expert-non-expert agreement rates on MT ranking tasks under different weighting schemes (uniform, weighted by experts, weighted by non-experts), with a 1/3 chance baseline marked. (Right) Bar chart comparing BLEU scores of translations from AMT non-experts vs. LCD professional translators across multiple MT systems.

image_metadata.caption: Expert–non-expert annotation agreement varies substantially across conversation topics. (Wang et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing sources of rater disagreement and the descriptive annotation paradigm; effective for motivating why demographic and topic metadata should be collected alongside labels.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap or scatter/bar chart showing Pearson or Spearman correlation coefficients between non-expert crowdworker annotations and expert (Trust & Safety professional) annotations, broken down by conversation topic, revealing high variance across topics.

image_metadata.caption: Taxonomy of root causes for rater disagreement in human annotation. (Zhang et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the rater disagreement section; revisit when explaining disagreement deconvolution and the descriptive vs. prescriptive annotation paradigms.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical taxonomy tree diagram categorizing causes of rater disagreement into major branches such as task/item difficulty, annotator characteristics (identity, knowledge, values), and stochastic/random errors, with leaf-level examples for each.

image_metadata.caption: Four architectures for learning from multi-annotator labels on a shared BERT encoder. (Davani et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to model annotator disagreement computationally; use to motivate the multi-task architecture before presenting its F1 and uncertainty estimation results.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing four model setups for handling multi-annotator labels: (1) single model trained on majority vote, (2) single model trained on individual labels, (3) multi-task model with one head per annotator sharing a common encoder, and (4) a variant thereof — all built on a shared BERT backbone.

image_metadata.caption: Jury learning aggregates predictions from demographically specified annotator 'jurors.' (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from passive disagreement modeling to active jury composition; effective for discussions on fairness and whose values a model should reflect.
image_metadata.description: A conceptual illustration of the jury learning framework: individual annotators with demographic characteristics are treated as 'jurors'; a practitioner specifies a jury composition; the model samples predictions from each juror and aggregates them into a final verdict.

image_metadata.caption: DCN architecture jointly embeds comment text, annotator identity, and demographics for jury learning. (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the jury learning concept when students need to understand the technical implementation; useful for students with a deep learning background who want architectural details.
image_metadata.description: A detailed neural network architecture diagram of the Deep & Cross Network (DCN) used for jury learning, showing parallel input streams for comment text (processed by a pre-trained language model), annotator embedding, and group/demographic embedding, which are combined through cross and deep layers before a final prediction head.

image_metadata.caption: Jury composition significantly shifts model predictions compared to an annotator-agnostic baseline. (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the jury learning architecture to show its practical impact; effective for motivating discussions on fairness and representation in annotation pipelines.
image_metadata.description: Bar or line charts comparing jury learning against an annotator-agnostic baseline across multiple jury compositions (e.g., all-male, all-female, balanced, random), showing differences in predicted toxicity rates or F1 scores, demonstrating that jury composition meaningfully shifts model outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Influence function estimates closely match leave-one-out retraining on MNIST. (Kohn & Liang 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing influence functions as a data quality tool; use to justify why the approximation is trustworthy before discussing its application to detecting mislabeled or low-quality training examples.
image_metadata.description: A scatter plot comparing influence function estimates of the effect of removing each training point against actual leave-one-out retraining results on 10-class MNIST, showing strong linear correlation and validating the closed-form approximation.

image_metadata.caption: Data map of SNLI training set partitioned by confidence and variability. (Swayamdipta et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this when explaining the Data Maps method (Swayamdipta et al. 2020) — show it to anchor the abstract concepts of 'confidence' and 'variability' to a concrete visual partition of a real dataset before discussing how these regions relate to data quality.
image_metadata.description: A 2D scatter plot (data map) of the SNLI training set using a RoBERTa classifier, with axes for 'variability' (x-axis) and 'confidence' (y-axis). Points are colored by region: easy-to-learn (high confidence, low variability), ambiguous (high variability), and hard-to-learn (low confidence, low variability). Density contours show clustering of samples in each region.

image_metadata.caption: Label-flipped points shift to low-confidence regions, validating data map mislabel detection. (Swayamdipta et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the data map figure (Image 1) when explaining how the hard-to-learn region relates to mislabeling — use it as empirical validation of the Data Maps hypothesis before moving to other detection methods.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side or overlaid data map scatter plot showing how data points shift in the confidence-variability space after their labels are artificially flipped. Originally high-confidence, low-variability (easy-to-learn) points migrate toward low-confidence, slightly higher-variability regions after label flipping, visually confirming that mislabeled samples appear in the hard-to-learn region.

image_metadata.caption: Threshold samples calibrate AUM cutoff to separate mislabeled from clean data. (Pleiss et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the AUM method and the threshold-sample trick — show it after defining the margin formula to make the threshold-setting mechanism concrete before presenting experimental results.
image_metadata.description: A diagram or plot illustrating how artificially inserted 'threshold samples' (with known incorrect labels) are used in the Area Under the Margin (AUM) method to set a decision boundary separating mislabeled from correctly labeled samples. Shows AUM score distributions for clean, mislabeled, and threshold samples, demonstrating that threshold samples help calibrate the cutoff.

image_metadata.caption: AUM filtering vs. other methods on CIFAR-10/100 with varying label noise rates. (Pleiss et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining both AUM and NCV methods — use it to compare methods empirically and prompt discussion about which approach is most practical and why performance varies with noise rate.
image_metadata.description: A bar or line chart comparing test error on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 benchmarks under varying levels of randomly mislabeled training data, contrasting AUM-based data filtering against other methods such as NCV, MentorNet, and standard training with noisy labels.

image_metadata.caption: INCV algorithm iteratively removes mislabeled samples via cross-validated label agreement. (Chen et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from NCV to INCV — show it to make the iterative refinement process concrete, especially when students ask how the method handles high noise rates where a single pass is insufficient.
image_metadata.description: A pseudocode or flowchart of the INCV (Iterative Noisy Cross-Validation) algorithm, showing the iterative process: splitting data into halves, training on each half, identifying 'clean' samples whose labels match cross-validated predictions, removing likely noisy samples, and repeating until convergence.

image_metadata.caption: Transfer learning transforms NLP, mirroring ImageNet's impact on vision. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on BERT or NLP transfer learning to motivate why these models matter and situate them historically.
image_metadata.description: A timeline or overview diagram showing the transfer learning paradigm in NLP, illustrating how pre-trained models (like BERT, ELMo, ULMFiT) represent an inflection point in NLP, analogous to ImageNet's impact on computer vision.

image_metadata.caption: BERT fine-tuned for spam classification with a classifier head. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing how BERT is applied to downstream tasks, before diving into architecture details, to ground students in practical usage.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing BERT used for spam classification: input text flows into the BERT model, and a classifier head on top outputs a spam/not-spam prediction.

image_metadata.caption: BERT Base vs. BERT Large: encoder layers, hidden units, attention heads. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing BERT's architecture section to help students distinguish the two model sizes and understand the role of hyperparameters.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison of BERT Base and BERT Large model sizes, showing differences in number of encoder layers, hidden units, and attention heads.

image_metadata.caption: BERT's stacked encoder blocks: 12 (Base) vs. 24 (Large) layers. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show alongside the model architecture explanation to help students visualize the stacking of encoder blocks and compare depths between BERT variants.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing the stacked encoder layers inside BERT Base (12 layers) and BERT Large (24 layers), visualizing the depth of the Transformer encoder stack.

image_metadata.caption: BERT input sequence with [CLS] token produces per-token output vectors. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining BERT's input requirements and output structure, especially before discussing how the [CLS] output is used for classification.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing BERT's input-output structure: a sequence of word tokens (with [CLS] as the first token) fed into the encoder stack, producing a corresponding sequence of output vectors.

image_metadata.caption: Tokens flow through BERT's encoder layers via self-attention and feed-forward networks. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the internal mechanics of BERT's encoder stack to illustrate how information flows and transforms through the network.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating how input tokens flow upward through BERT's stacked encoder layers, with each layer applying self-attention and feed-forward operations before passing results to the next layer.

image_metadata.caption: BERT's [CLS] output: a 768-dim vector fed to the classifier. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how BERT's output is connected to a classifier, particularly to clarify why only the [CLS] position output is used for sentence-level tasks.
image_metadata.description: A diagram highlighting the output vector at the [CLS] position (first token) from BERT's final encoder layer, showing it as a 768-dimensional vector used as input to a downstream classifier.

image_metadata.caption: A simple single-layer classifier on BERT's output achieves strong results. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing fine-tuning and the classifier architecture to emphasize how lightweight the task-specific component is relative to the pre-trained BERT model.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing a single-layer neural network classifier receiving the [CLS] output vector from BERT and producing classification probabilities (e.g., spam/not spam) via softmax.

image_metadata.caption: VGGNet's conv+classifier mirrors BERT's encoder+head structure. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when drawing the parallel between BERT and CNNs to help students with vision backgrounds understand the transfer learning paradigm by analogy.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of VGGNet architecture showing the convolutional feature extraction layers followed by fully-connected classification layers, used as an analogy to BERT + classifier head.

image_metadata.caption: GloVe assigns fixed vectors to words, ignoring context. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing contextual embeddings (ELMo, BERT) to contrast static word vectors with context-dependent representations and motivate the need for models like BERT.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing GloVe/Word2Vec-style static word embeddings, where each word maps to a fixed vector regardless of context, illustrating the traditional word representation approach.

image_metadata.caption: A word represented as a dense numeric vector (embedding). — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any lesson on word representations or before introducing Word2Vec/GloVe to establish the visual language used throughout the article.
image_metadata.description: A visual representation of a word embedding as a colored rectangular vector box, showing how a word maps to a list of numbers (a dense vector).

image_metadata.caption: ELMo assigns different embeddings to 'stick' based on context. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of polysemy and the motivation for contextualized embeddings, after students understand static embeddings.
image_metadata.description: Diagram contrasting a static GloVe embedding for the word 'stick' with ELMo's context-sensitive embedding, illustrating that ELMo assigns different vectors to the same word depending on surrounding context.

image_metadata.caption: GloVe gives 'stick' one fixed vector regardless of meaning. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the limitation of fixed embeddings, just before introducing ELMo and contextualized representations.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of a GloVe word embedding for the word 'stick' as a long vector of numbers, demonstrating the fixed, context-independent nature of static word embeddings.

image_metadata.caption: ELMo's bi-directional LSTM hidden states power its embeddings. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining ELMo's pre-training mechanism and how hidden states from an LSTM become the basis for contextualized embeddings.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of ELMo's bi-directional LSTM architecture performing language modeling, showing hidden states emerging from each LSTM step and being used to form embeddings.

image_metadata.caption: ELMo trains forward and backward LSTMs simultaneously for context. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the difference between unidirectional and bidirectional language models, and how ELMo's training objective differs from GPT.
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram of ELMo's forward and backward language model pre-training, showing the input sequence 'Let's stick to' with the model predicting the next word in both directions.

image_metadata.caption: ELMo combines all LSTM layer hidden states into one embedding. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining ELMo's LSTM architecture, when discussing how the pre-trained hidden states are converted into usable word embeddings for downstream tasks.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how ELMo combines hidden states from multiple LSTM layers (via concatenation and weighted summation) to produce a final contextualized word embedding.

image_metadata.caption: GPT stacks 12 Transformer decoder layers for language modeling. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing GPT and contrasting it with the full encoder-decoder Transformer, emphasizing the architectural choice of using only the decoder.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of the OpenAI GPT model showing a stack of 12 Transformer decoder layers (without encoder-decoder attention) used for language model pre-training.

image_metadata.caption: GPT uses masked self-attention to predict the next word. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how GPT's masked self-attention differs from BERT's bidirectional attention, and why masking is necessary for language modeling.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the OpenAI Transformer performing language modeling pre-training, showing masked self-attention processing a sequence of tokens to predict the next word.

image_metadata.caption: Fine-tuning GPT for sentence classification adds one output layer. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the concept of fine-tuning pre-trained models for downstream tasks, as a concrete example before discussing BERT's approach.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing how the pre-trained OpenAI Transformer is fine-tuned for sentence classification by adding a classification layer on top of the final decoder output.

image_metadata.caption: GPT reformats diverse NLP tasks as token sequences with delimiters. — Jay Alammar
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how GPT handles multiple NLP tasks with a single architecture, and as a contrast to BERT's approach using [CLS] and [SEP] tokens.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multiple input transformation schemes for the OpenAI Transformer, illustrating how different NLP tasks (classification, entailment, similarity, multiple choice) are reformatted as linear token sequences with special delimiter tokens.

image_metadata.caption: BERT's Masked LM: predict hidden tokens using bidirectional context. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When explaining why BERT uses masking instead of standard left-to-right language modeling; use to contrast with GPT's unidirectional approach
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing BERT's Masked Language Model (MLM) pre-training objective, where 15% of input tokens are masked with [MASK] tokens and the model must predict the original words from bidirectional context

image_metadata.caption: BERT's Next Sentence Prediction pre-training task for sentence-pair understanding. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When introducing BERT's two pre-training tasks; pair with the MLM diagram to give a complete picture of BERT's pre-training strategy before discussing fine-tuning
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating BERT's Next Sentence Prediction (NSP) pre-training task, showing two input sentences (A and B) fed into BERT with a binary classification output indicating whether B follows A

image_metadata.caption: BERT fine-tuned for four distinct NLP task types with minimal modification. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: After explaining pre-training; use when discussing how one model generalizes across NLP benchmarks, and to contrast fine-tuning with feature extraction
image_metadata.description: Grid or panel diagram showing how BERT is adapted for multiple downstream NLP tasks (e.g., classification, NER, question answering, sentence-pair tasks) by adding task-specific output layers on top of the pre-trained model

image_metadata.caption: Comparing BERT layer extraction strategies for contextualized embeddings on NER. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When contrasting fine-tuning vs. feature extraction use of BERT; use to prompt discussion about what different transformer layers encode
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing different strategies for extracting contextualized embeddings from BERT's layers (e.g., last layer, second-to-last layer, sum of last four layers, concatenation of last four layers) with performance scores on a benchmark task

image_metadata.caption: BERT as a frozen feature extractor feeding embeddings into a downstream model. (Alammar)
image_metadata.when_to_show: When teaching the two modes of using pre-trained models (fine-tuning vs. feature extraction); effective for students familiar with ELMo who are learning how BERT extends that paradigm
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the feature extraction pipeline where a frozen pre-trained BERT model generates contextualized token embeddings that are then fed into a separate downstream model (e.g., for named entity recognition)

image_metadata.caption: Skip-gram model: one-hot input → N-dim embedding → softmax output. (Weng, 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the skip-gram model, specifically when explaining how a single target word is used to predict a context word and how the hidden layer encodes the word embedding of size N.
image_metadata.description: Neural network architecture diagram of the skip-gram model showing a one-hot encoded input vector x of size V, a hidden layer of size N representing the word embedding, weight matrices W (V×N) and W' (N×V), and a softmax output layer producing a one-hot encoded output vector y of size V.

image_metadata.caption: CBOW model: multiple context vectors averaged → predict target word. (Weng, 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the skip-gram diagram when comparing the two word2vec architectures, emphasizing the reversed prediction direction (context → target) and the averaging of multiple input vectors in the hidden layer.
image_metadata.description: Neural network architecture diagram of the CBOW model showing multiple one-hot encoded context word input vectors, individual projections through weight matrix W into N-dimensional vectors, an averaging operation in the hidden layer to produce a single fixed-length vector, and a softmax output layer predicting the target word.

image_metadata.caption: Hierarchical softmax tree: O(log V) path replaces full softmax. (Weng, 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining why standard softmax is computationally impractical for large vocabularies and introducing hierarchical softmax as a solution, using the path notation n(w_i, j) to walk through how a single word's probability is computed.
image_metadata.description: Binary tree diagram illustrating hierarchical softmax, with gray internal nodes representing probability branch points, white leaf nodes representing vocabulary words, and a highlighted path from the root to a specific leaf word w_i, with nodes along the path labeled n(w_i, j) for the j-th node.

lessons.title: LoRA & Parameter-Efficient Methods
lessons.content:
# LoRA & Parameter-Efficient Methods

Fine-tuning a big LLM the “classic” way means updating *every* weight. That’s like repainting an entire house because you want one room to be a different color: it works, but it’s expensive, slow, and you end up storing a whole new copy of the model for each task.

**Parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT)** flips the idea: keep the base model frozen, and learn a small set of extra parameters that *steer* it.

## The baseline: full fine-tuning is “W + ΔW”

In a Transformer, many of the heavy hitters are big matrices (especially in attention projections). In full fine-tuning, each weight matrix **W** gets updated by a dense matrix **ΔW**.

![Standard finetuning updates full weight matrix W with ΔW. (Raschka, 2023)](/api/wiki-images/lora-peft/images/sebastianraschka-blog-2023-llm-finetuning-lora-html_002.png)

A useful mental model is: you’re learning a *direction* to move each matrix so the model behaves differently on your task.

## LoRA: learn a low-rank ΔW instead of a full one

**LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation)** makes one surgical change: it assumes the update **ΔW** doesn’t need to be full-rank. Instead, it can be expressed as the product of two skinny matrices:

\[
\Delta W \approx B A
\]

where **A** and **B** have rank **r** (and *r is small*, like 8 or 16).

![LoRA decomposes ΔW into two small matrices W_A and W_B. (Raschka, 2023)](/api/wiki-images/lora-peft/images/sebastianraschka-blog-2023-llm-finetuning-lora-html_004.png)

Why this is such a big deal:
- A full update for a \(d\times d\) matrix costs \(d^2\) parameters.
- A low-rank update costs about \(2dr\) parameters.
- If \(r \ll d\), you often end up training **<1%** of the model.

During training, the base weights stay frozen; only the LoRA matrices learn. At inference, you can “merge” the update into W (or keep it separate) without adding extra latency in the common setup.

## Practical knobs that matter (more than you’d think)

When people say “I trained a LoRA,” they’re really making a few key choices:

- **target modules**: which matrices get LoRA (e.g., attention projections). A strong default is to apply LoRA broadly across attention projections (not only a subset).
- **rank (r)**: capacity of the adapter. Too small underfits; too large wastes compute.
- **alpha (lora_alpha)**: scales the LoRA update. A common heuristic is **alpha ≈ 2r**.
- **epochs**: with a static dataset, doing many passes can overfit surprisingly fast.

## QLoRA: LoRA on top of a 4-bit frozen base model

**QLoRA** combines two ideas:
1. Quantize the *frozen* base model weights to **4-bit** to save VRAM.
2. Train LoRA adapters in higher precision while backpropagating through the quantized model.

This is how you can fine-tune very large models on a single GPU that would otherwise be out of reach. The trade-off is typically **less memory** but **more runtime**.

![LoRA adapters enable fine-tuning of frozen 4-bit quantized base models. (Hugging Face Blog)](/api/wiki-images/lora-peft/images/huggingface-co-blog-4bit-transformers-bitsandbytes_002.gif)

## Minimal PEFT + (Q)LoRA setup (Transformers + PEFT)

```python
import torch
from transformers import AutoModelForCausalLM, BitsAndBytesConfig
from peft import LoraConfig, get_peft_model, prepare_model_for_kbit_training

model_id = "mistralai/Mistral-7B-v0.1"

bnb_config = BitsAndBytesConfig(
    load_in_4bit=True,
    bnb_4bit_quant_type="nf4",
    bnb_4bit_use_double_quant=True,
    bnb_4bit_compute_dtype=torch.bfloat16,
)

base = AutoModelForCausalLM.from_pretrained(model_id, quantization_config=bnb_config)
base = prepare_model_for_kbit_training(base)

lora_cfg = LoraConfig(
    r=16,
    lora_alpha=32,
    lora_dropout=0.05,
    target_modules=["q_proj", "k_proj", "v_proj", "o_proj"],
    bias="none",
    task_type="CAUSAL_LM",
)

model = get_peft_model(base, lora_cfg)
model.print_trainable_parameters()
```

The key pattern: **load quantized → prepare for k-bit training → attach LoRA → train only adapters → save tiny adapter weights**.

## Summary

**LoRA** gets most of the benefit of fine-tuning by learning a low-rank weight update while keeping the base model frozen, slashing trainable parameters and storage. **QLoRA** pushes this further by quantizing the frozen base model (often to 4-bit) so you can fine-tune much larger models within limited GPU memory.

## Recommended Reading
- [LoRA: Low-Rank Adaptation of Large Language Models](https://arxiv.org/abs/2106.09685) — The original method and why low-rank updates work.
- [Practical Tips for Finetuning LLMs Using LoRA](https://magazine.sebastianraschka.com/p/practical-tips-for-finetuning-llms) — Battle-tested heuristics (rank, alpha, layer coverage, overfitting).
- [Making LLMs even more accessible with bitsandbytes, 4-bit quantization and QLoRA](https://huggingface.co/blog/4bit-transformers-bitsandbytes) — Intuition + tooling for 4-bit + adapters.
- [PEFT Quantization Guide](https://huggingface.co/docs/peft/main/en/developer_guides/quantization) — Step-by-step recipe for QLoRA-style training.
lessons.summary: LoRA fine-tunes large LLMs by learning low-rank weight updates (tiny adapter matrices) while freezing the base model, achieving near full fine-tuning quality with a fraction of trainable parameters. QLoRA adds 4-bit quantization of the frozen base model to dramatically reduce VRAM, enabling much larger-model fine-tuning on a single GPU.
lessons.video_title: LoRA and QLoRA Explained (Low Rank Adaptation and Quantized LoRA)

image_metadata.caption: LoRA adapters enable fine-tuning of frozen 4-bit quantized base models. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how 4-bit or 8-bit quantized models can still be fine-tuned, particularly when discussing RLHF use cases where multiple adapters (reward model, value policy) are trained on a single quantized base model.
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF illustrating the LoRA (Low-Rank Adaptation) mechanism, showing how small trainable adapter matrices are added on top of a frozen base model, with the animation likely depicting the forward pass through frozen weights and the parallel low-rank update path.

image_metadata.caption: LoRA decomposes ΔW into two small matrices W_A and W_B. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: This is the central diagram — show it when formally introducing LoRA after the motivation diagrams, and return to it when discussing parameter counts and storage savings.
image_metadata.description: Core LoRA architecture diagram showing the original frozen weight matrix W alongside two small trainable matrices W_A (A×r) and W_B (r×B) whose product approximates ΔW, with rank r much smaller than A or B.

image_metadata.caption: LoRA matches or beats full finetuning on NLP benchmarks. (LoRA paper, via Raschka 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the LoRA mechanism, when students ask 'but does it actually work?' — use it to discuss trade-offs between efficiency and performance.
image_metadata.description: Annotated table from the original LoRA paper comparing ROUGE scores of LoRA against full finetuning, adapter methods, prompt tuning, and prefix tuning across multiple NLP benchmarks, showing LoRA often matches or exceeds full finetuning.

image_metadata.caption: FP8 bit layout: E4M3 vs E5M2 formats showing sign, exponent, and mantissa. (HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing quantization formats, specifically when explaining why different FP8 variants (E4M3 vs E5M2) exist and how bit allocation determines representable range (-448 to 448 for E4M3) versus precision.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the FP8 floating point format, showing the bit-level breakdown of sign, exponent, and mantissa fields for both E4M3 and E5M2 variants, with labeled bit positions and their roles in representing floating point numbers.

image_metadata.caption: PCA illustrates low-rank approximation: the core idea behind LoRA. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this before explaining LoRA math — use it to build intuition that high-dimensional data/matrices often have a lower-dimensional structure that captures most information.
image_metadata.description: A PCA/SVD visualization showing how a high-dimensional dataset can be approximated by a lower-dimensional representation, illustrating the conceptual foundation of low-rank decomposition with axes and projected data points.

image_metadata.caption: Standard finetuning updates full weight matrix W with ΔW. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show before introducing LoRA decomposition — students need to understand the standard update rule to appreciate why decomposing ΔW saves parameters.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of standard full finetuning showing a weight matrix W being updated to W' = W + ΔW, with input x and output h, illustrating how the full weight update matrix is applied during conventional finetuning.

image_metadata.caption: Separating ΔW from W enables the LoRA low-rank substitution. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the standard finetuning diagram, as a bridge to LoRA — emphasize that this separation is what allows ΔW to be replaced by a low-rank product.
image_metadata.description: Alternative formulation diagram showing h = Wx + ΔWx, where the original frozen weight W and the update ΔW are kept separate and both applied to input x, with outputs summed.

image_metadata.caption: Alpaca 52k instruction dataset format used for LoRA-LLaMA finetuning. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the computational benchmarks section, before discussing training results — students need to understand the data format to contextualize the experiments.
image_metadata.description: Example of the Alpaca 52k instruction dataset format, showing the structure of a training example with instruction, input, and output fields used for instruction finetuning of LLaMA.

image_metadata.caption: Self-Instruct 4-step pipeline generates instruction data automatically. (Raschka, 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when students ask where the Alpaca training data comes from, or when discussing data efficiency and the cost of creating instruction-following datasets.
image_metadata.description: Four-step Self-Instruct pipeline diagram showing how instruction-following datasets are automatically generated: (1) seed tasks, (2) instruction generation via LLM, (3) instance generation, (4) filtering and post-processing.

image_metadata.caption: Multi-head scaled dot-product attention splits inputs across parallel heads. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the multi-head attention mechanism, especially to contrast single-head vs multi-head attention and explain the concatenation + projection step.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing multi-head scaled dot-product attention: input splits into h heads, each computing scaled dot-product attention in parallel, outputs concatenated and linearly projected.

image_metadata.caption: Vanilla Transformer encoder-decoder architecture with attention and feed-forward layers. (Vaswani et al., 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any Transformer overview lesson or when transitioning from individual components to the full model, as a reference map students can return to.
image_metadata.description: Full architecture diagram of the vanilla Transformer model showing the encoder stack (left) and decoder stack (right), including multi-head attention, feed-forward layers, add & norm, positional encoding, and cross-attention.
