image_metadata.caption: Sinusoidal positional encoding heatmap for L=32, d=128; values range −1 to 1.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the positional encoding formula to give students an intuition for what the encoding looks like and why different positions produce distinguishable patterns.
image_metadata.description: Heatmap visualization of sinusoidal positional encoding for sequence length L=32 and embedding dimension d=128, showing alternating sine/cosine patterns across positions and dimensions, with values ranging from -1 (black) to 1 (white).

image_metadata.caption: RoPE encodes position as rotation in 2D subspaces of the embedding. (Su et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching relative positional encodings, especially after sinusoidal encoding, to contrast additive vs rotational approaches and explain RoPE's advantages for long-context tasks.
image_metadata.description: Visual illustration of Rotary Position Embedding (RoPE) showing how token embeddings at position i are rotated in 2D subspaces using a rotation matrix, with the rotation angle proportional to position index.

image_metadata.caption: Transformer-XL caches prior segment states to extend attention span. (Dai et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining the context segmentation problem in vanilla Transformers and how Transformer-XL's recurrence mechanism solves it.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side comparison of training phases for vanilla Transformer (left) and Transformer-XL (right) with segment length 4, showing how Transformer-XL caches previous segment hidden states to extend the attention span across segments.

image_metadata.caption: Compressive Transformer uses regular and compressed memory for long context. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after Transformer-XL to demonstrate how compressive memory further extends context, and when discussing tradeoffs between memory size and temporal range.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of the Compressive Transformer showing two memory types: regular memory (recent past activations) and compressed memory (older activations compressed by a compression function), with attention attending to both.

image_metadata.caption: Attention weights across memory positions show compressed memory is actively used. (Rae et al., 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing whether compression loses important information, as empirical evidence that the model attends to compressed memory and it is not wasted.
image_metadata.description: Line plot showing attention weights (with one standard deviation error bars) versus memory positions from oldest (left) to newest (right) in the Compressive Transformer, demonstrating that compressed memory receives meaningful attention despite being older.

image_metadata.caption: SPALM combines recurrent short-term and k-NN long-term memory via gating. (Yogatama et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching non-differentiable external memory augmentation, especially to contrast with purely recurrent approaches and explain how retrieval-augmented memory works.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of SPALM architecture showing how short-term memory (past hidden states from Transformer-XL-style cache) and long-term memory (external k-NN key-value datastore) are combined via a gating mechanism to produce the final output.

image_metadata.caption: Fine-tuning with k-NN memory matches scratch-trained Memorizing Transformer performance. (Wu et al., 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing practical deployment of memory-augmented Transformers, particularly to motivate fine-tuning as a cheaper alternative to full retraining.
image_metadata.description: Line chart comparing perplexity vs training steps for: training a memorizing transformer from scratch, fine-tuning a vanilla Transformer with k-NN memory, and a baseline vanilla Transformer, showing fine-tuning achieves comparable performance.

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi adds a negative linear distance bias to attention scores per head. (Press et al., 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching distance-enhanced attention methods, especially to contrast with RoPE and learned positional encodings, and when discussing context length extrapolation strategies.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating ALiBi (Attention with Linear Biases) showing how a negative linear bias proportional to the distance between query and key positions is added to attention scores before softmax, with different slopes per head.

image_metadata.caption: ALiBi extrapolates better than sinusoidal, rotary, and T5 encodings. (Press et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing ALiBi or comparing positional encoding schemes; use to motivate why additive linear biases outperform learned or sinusoidal encodings for out-of-distribution sequence lengths.
image_metadata.description: Line graphs comparing extrapolation performance of four positional encoding schemes (sinusoidal, rotary, T5 relative, ALiBi) when models trained on short context lengths are tested on longer sequences. ALiBi shows the flattest perplexity curve as context length grows beyond training length.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer refines all positions in parallel across recurrent steps. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Universal Transformer to illustrate how recurrence replaces fixed-depth stacking; contrast with standard Transformer layer diagram to highlight the iterative refinement concept.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the Universal Transformer's recurrent refinement loop: a sequence of token representations is updated repeatedly across multiple time steps in parallel, with each step applying self-attention and a transition function to all positions simultaneously.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Transformer encoder-decoder with shared recurrent weights and cross-attention. (Dehghani et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after the loop diagram (Image 2) to give the full encoder-decoder picture; useful when comparing Universal Transformer to standard seq2seq Transformers.
image_metadata.description: Simplified encoder-decoder diagram of Universal Transformer showing the shared recurrent structure in both encoder and decoder, with the decoder additionally attending to the final encoder representation h^T via cross-attention.

image_metadata.caption: Soft mask function controls adaptive attention span boundary via learnable parameter z. (Sukhbaatar et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after introducing the m_z formula to help students visualize what 'soft' means in this context and how z controls the effective span boundary.
image_metadata.description: Plot of the soft masking function m_z used in adaptive attention span, showing how it maps query-key distance to a [0,1] value with a smooth sigmoid-shaped transition controlled by parameter z, effectively creating a soft boundary for the attention window.

image_metadata.caption: Three adaptive depth classifier types: sequence-specific, token-specific, and hybrid. (Elabyad et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching adaptive depth or conditional computation; use to compare granularity levels before discussing CALM, and to motivate why token-level exits are more flexible but harder to train.
image_metadata.description: Illustration of three depth-adaptive classifier configurations: (1) sequence-specific — one exit shared by all tokens in a sequence; (2) token-specific — each token has its own exit; (3) a hybrid approach. Diagrams show how classifiers attach to different transformer layers.

image_metadata.caption: 1D vs 2D local attention spans for pixel query q in Image Transformer. (Parmer et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing local/fixed-context attention mechanisms, especially in the context of image generation; use to contrast with full self-attention before discussing efficiency gains.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side diagrams of 1D and 2D local attention patterns for image generation in Image Transformer. Each shows a query block (black outline) and the corresponding attention span (cyan outline) for a query pixel q, illustrating how context is restricted to a local neighborhood.

image_metadata.caption: Full vs. strided vs. fixed sparse attention patterns and their connectivity matrices. (Child et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing sparse attention mechanisms; use the matrix view to quantify sparsity and the connectivity diagram to explain how information still flows between distant tokens through multi-hop paths.
image_metadata.description: Two-row figure comparing attention connectivity for (a) full Transformer, (b) Sparse Transformer with strided attention, and (c) Sparse Transformer with fixed attention. Top row shows which tokens attend to which in a sequence diagram; bottom row shows the corresponding sparse attention matrices.

image_metadata.caption: Attention patterns compared: ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird global-local combinations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing efficient long-sequence transformer architectures; use as a summary diagram after covering each model individually to reinforce similarities and differences.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side attention pattern diagrams for ETC, Longformer, and Big Bird, each showing how global tokens, local sliding-window attention, and random attention are combined. Color-coded blocks indicate different attention types (global, local, random).

image_metadata.caption: LSH attention produces block-diagonal sparse matrix by attending within hash buckets. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the LSH bucketing process (Image 10) to illustrate the resulting attention matrix structure; use to connect the hashing algorithm to the actual computation savings.
image_metadata.description: Visualization of the LSH attention matrix after bucketing and sorting: tokens are grouped into hash buckets (shown by colored blocks along the diagonal), and attention is only computed within each bucket, producing a block-diagonal sparse attention matrix.

image_metadata.caption: Four steps of LSH attention: bucket, sort, chunk, then attend within chunks. (Kitaev et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing Reformer's LSH attention mechanism; walk through each of the four steps sequentially before showing the resulting attention matrix (Image 9).
image_metadata.description: Step-by-step diagram of the four LSH attention stages: (1) bucketing tokens by hash, (2) sorting tokens by bucket assignment, (3) chunking sorted tokens into fixed-size blocks, and (4) computing attention only within each chunk. Uses color coding to track token assignments.

image_metadata.caption: Linformer projects keys/values to rank-k, achieving linear attention complexity. (Wang et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing Linformer after explaining the quadratic cost of standard attention; use the right panel to motivate why linear approximations matter at scale (e.g., L > 1024).
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) architecture diagram of Linformer showing linear projection layers E and F added to keys and values to reduce sequence dimension from L to k; (Right) log-scale plot comparing inference time vs. sequence length for Linformer versus standard Transformer, showing Linformer's linear scaling advantage.

image_metadata.caption: RFA reorders attention computation via kernel features, avoiding the quadratic attention matrix. (Peng et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RFA kernel approximation formula φ(x)·φ(y) ≈ exp(x·y); use it to explain why reordering (K^T V first) is valid and how it achieves linear complexity.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel diagram contrasting computation order: (Left) standard softmax attention computes the full L×L attention matrix before multiplying by values; (Right) Random Feature Attention (RFA) reorders operations using kernel approximation φ(·), computing key-value products first to avoid the quadratic matrix, enabling linear-time attention.

image_metadata.caption: Orthogonal and positive random features reduce approximation error in Performer's FAVOR+. (Choromanski et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Performer's FAVOR+ after explaining the general random feature approach; use to justify why orthogonal and positive features are preferred over naive i.i.d. or sin/cos alternatives.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel comparison of approximation error for Performer's FAVOR+ mechanism: (Left) i.i.d. random features vs. orthogonal random features showing lower variance with orthogonal sampling; (Right) sin/cos random features vs. positive random features showing positive features yield lower approximation error for softmax kernel estimation.

image_metadata.caption: GTrXL adds gating units and reorders layer norm to stabilize Transformer training in RL. (Parisotto et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing GTrXL as a solution to Transformer training instability in RL; use to highlight the specific architectural differences (gate placement, norm reordering) before discussing empirical RL results.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams of three variants: (1) standard Transformer-XL with layer norm before residual addition; (2) Transformer-XL with reordered layer norm (pre-norm); (3) Gated Transformer-XL (GTrXL) replacing residual connections with gating units (GRU-style gates) and using the reordered layer norm, enabling stable training for RL.

lessons.title: Evaluation & Benchmarks
lessons.content:
# Evaluation & Benchmarks

You can’t improve what you can’t measure—but with LLMs, “measure” is slippery.

A model can ace a multiple-choice exam and still be a frustrating chatbot. It can write beautiful prose that’s subtly wrong. It can look safe… until someone tries to jailbreak it. So modern LLM evaluation is less like a single final exam and more like a **dashboard**: multiple instruments, each with blind spots.

## 1) Standardized benchmarks: the “unit tests” of LLMs

A **benchmark** is a fixed dataset + scoring rule. The appeal is obvious: it’s cheap, repeatable, and gives you a number.

Two famous examples:

- **MMLU**: 57 subject areas (math, history, law, CS, …) scored mostly as multiple-choice accuracy. It’s a broad “academic knowledge + reasoning” check.
- **HumanEval**: code-generation problems scored by running unit tests against the model’s generated function.

In practice, teams rarely run just one benchmark. They run *suites* (dozens to hundreds of tasks) using frameworks like LM Evaluation Harness.

## 2) Benchmark scores are not as comparable as they look

A subtle trap: “MMLU = 70%” is not a universal constant. Small differences in **prompting**, **few-shot examples**, and even **answer extraction logic** can move scores.

![Same MMLU question, three different prompts across benchmark implementations. (Adapted from Eugene Yan)](/api/wiki-images/evaluation-benchmarks/images/eugeneyan-writing-llm-patterns_004.webp)

![Evaluation logic differs across MMLU implementations, not just prompts. (Adapted from Eugene Yan)](/api/wiki-images/evaluation-benchmarks/images/eugeneyan-writing-llm-patterns_005.webp)

Takeaway: treat leaderboards as *signals*, not ground truth. If you’re making a product decision, rerun evals in *your* exact setup.

## 3) LLM-as-judge: scalable “taste tests” for open-ended outputs

For tasks like summarization, chat helpfulness, or writing quality, there often isn’t a single correct answer. That’s where **LLM-as-judge** comes in: use a strong model to score or compare outputs.

A common pattern is pairwise comparison (“Which answer is better?”), which tends to be more reliable than absolute 1–10 ratings.

![G-Eval uses CoT and token probabilities for reference-free LLM evaluation. (Adapted from OpenAI Cookbook)](/api/wiki-images/evaluation-benchmarks/images/eugeneyan-writing-llm-patterns_006.webp)

Two well-known judge-based evaluation settings:

- **MT-Bench**: multi-turn questions scored by an LLM judge.
- **Chatbot Arena**: crowdsourced head-to-head battles; rankings emerge from many human votes.

Caveat: judges have biases (verbosity, position, self-preference). Good eval design tries to counteract these (randomization, calibration, multiple judges).

## 4) Human evaluation: slow, expensive, still the gold standard

Humans are essential when you care about real user preferences, nuanced safety, or domain correctness. But human eval is a *data collection pipeline*, not a button:

- clear rubrics and examples
- rater training + calibration
- aggregation to reduce noise and spam

This is why teams often use LLM-as-judge for iteration speed, then validate with humans for high-stakes decisions.

## 5) Red teaming: evaluation for the things you *hope* never happen

**Red teaming** is adversarial evaluation: actively trying to break the system (jailbreaks, prompt injection, data leakage, policy bypass). It’s less about average-case performance and more about worst-case behavior.

A useful mindset is to define a threat model: *who is attacking, what do they know, and what counts as failure?*

## 6) Contamination: when the test leaks into training

If benchmark questions (or close paraphrases) appear in training data, scores inflate. This is **contamination**. It’s especially tricky for popular public benchmarks that have been copied widely.

Practical response: use newer/held-out eval sets, track data provenance, and rely on multiple complementary eval types.

## Summary

LLM evaluation is a portfolio: standardized benchmarks for repeatable checks, LLM-as-judge and human studies for open-ended quality, and red teaming for adversarial safety. The most important skill isn’t memorizing scores—it’s designing evals you trust and rerunning them in your exact deployment setup.

## Recommended Reading
- [LM Evaluation Harness (tasks)](https://github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness/tree/main/lm_eval/tasks) — Browse the huge landscape of benchmark tasks and variants.
- [LM Evaluation Harness (repo)](https://github.com/EleutherAI/lm-evaluation-harness) — Practical tooling for running reproducible eval suites.
- [Judging LLM-as-a-Judge with MT-Bench and Chatbot Arena](https://arxiv.org/abs/2306.05685) — How judge models and crowdsourced battles approximate human preference.
- [How to eval abstractive summarization](https://cookbook.openai.com/examples/evaluation/how_to_eval_abstractive_summarization) — Traditional metrics vs LLM-based evaluation in a concrete workflow.
lessons.summary: LLM evaluation works best as a multi-tool: benchmarks for repeatable measurement, LLM-as-judge and human studies for open-ended quality, and red teaming for adversarial safety. Scores are only meaningful when you control for prompting, scoring logic, and contamination.

image_metadata.caption: Same MMLU question, three different prompts across benchmark implementations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing benchmark reliability or when students assume leaderboard scores are directly comparable across implementations.
image_metadata.description: A table or side-by-side comparison showing the same MMLU question formatted with different prompts across three implementations: original MMLU, HELM, and EleutherAI, highlighting prompt wording differences.

image_metadata.caption: Evaluation logic differs across MMLU implementations, not just prompts.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Image 5 to compound the lesson: both prompts AND evaluation logic differ, making cross-benchmark comparisons unreliable.
image_metadata.description: A comparison table or diagram showing how the evaluation methodology (e.g., answer extraction, scoring approach) differs for the same MMLU question across original, HELM, and EleutherAI implementations.

image_metadata.caption: G-Eval uses CoT and token probabilities for reference-free LLM evaluation.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing LLM-based evaluation frameworks, especially to contrast with traditional metrics like BLEU/ROUGE and human evaluation.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart overview of the G-Eval framework showing: (1) task introduction + criteria fed to LLM to generate Chain-of-Thought evaluation steps, (2) concatenation of prompt, CoT, article, and summary, (3) LLM outputs a score, and (4) token probabilities used for weighted score normalization.

image_metadata.caption: Qwen model card showing author-reported evaluation results on Hugging Face Hub.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching students the difference between author-reported evaluation scores on model cards versus community-driven leaderboard scores; use to illustrate how to read and critically assess model capability claims.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of a Qwen model card on Hugging Face Hub, showing evaluation results and metadata including benchmark scores, model capabilities, and performance metrics as reported by the model author.

image_metadata.caption: Taxonomy of adversarial threats to LLM applications. (Greshake et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on LLM security to orient students before introducing specific attack methods.
image_metadata.description: A taxonomy diagram showing the threat landscape for LLM-based applications, including prompt injection, jailbreaks, data extraction, and indirect attacks via external content sources like web pages and plugins.

image_metadata.caption: Probability density of Gumbel(0,1) used in softmax reparameterization. (ChatGPT)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Gumbel-softmax reparameterization trick, immediately before explaining how it enables differentiable approximation of discrete token sampling.
image_metadata.description: A probability density plot of the Gumbel(0,1) distribution, showing its characteristic right-skewed shape with a sharp peak near 0 and a long right tail.

image_metadata.caption: Gumbel-softmax at varying temperatures: τ→0 is categorical, τ→∞ is uniform. (Jang et al. 2016)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the Gumbel(0,1) density plot, when explaining how temperature τ controls the smoothness of the approximation and why τ→0 recovers the original discrete distribution.
image_metadata.description: A multi-panel figure from Jang et al. 2016 showing how Gumbel-softmax distributions behave at different temperatures (τ→0 approaches one-hot categorical; τ→∞ approaches uniform), plus plots comparing expectations and samples from the distribution.

image_metadata.caption: Universal Adversarial Triggers prepended to any input force targeted model outputs. (Wallace et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when first introducing UAT to illustrate the concept of a universal trigger before explaining the gradient-based search algorithm used to find it.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the Universal Adversarial Triggers (UAT) method: a short trigger phrase is prepended to any input, causing the model to produce a targeted incorrect output regardless of the actual input content.

image_metadata.caption: Example UAT triggers for sentiment, QA, and text generation tasks. (Wallace et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the UAT algorithm to ground the theory in real examples, and use the nonsensical trigger tokens to motivate the discussion of perplexity-based detection.
image_metadata.description: A table of example UAT trigger phrases for different NLP tasks (sentiment classification, reading comprehension, conditional text generation), showing the actual learned trigger tokens and the resulting model outputs.

image_metadata.caption: UTSC pipeline: unigram triggers → LLM generation → toxicity-filtered attack utterances. (Mehrabi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing UAT variants, specifically to contrast UTSC's multi-step pipeline with the simpler UAT approach and explain why lower perplexity matters for evading defenses.
image_metadata.description: A flowchart illustrating the UTSC (Unigram Trigger with Selection Criteria) pipeline: unigram UAT tokens are generated, passed with conversation history to an LLM to produce attack utterances, then filtered by toxicity classifiers.

image_metadata.caption: Attack success rates of UAT variants measured by multiple toxicity classifiers. (Mehrabi et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining all UAT variants to summarize their relative performance and prompt discussion about which metrics best capture attack quality.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing attack success rates of UAT, UAT-LM, UTSC-1, UTSC-2, and UTSC-3 as measured by multiple toxicity classifiers (Perspective API, Safety classifier, etc.) against a defender model.

image_metadata.caption: Red tokens mark adversarial suffix positions learned by GCG optimization. (Zou et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the Zou et al. GCG method to visually anchor where the learned adversarial tokens appear in the prompt before explaining the optimization procedure.
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing where adversarial suffix tokens (marked with red exclamation points) are appended to a user instruction in the prompt, illustrating the GCG (Greedy Coordinate Gradient) attack setup from Zou et al. 2023.

image_metadata.caption: GCG suffix attack success rates on Vicuna models; concatenation boosts performance. (Zou et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining the GCG optimization algorithm to present its empirical effectiveness and motivate discussion of why concatenation and ensemble strategies boost success rates.
image_metadata.description: A results table showing average attack success rates of GCG adversarial suffixes on Vicuna-7b and Vicuna-13b for harmful behavior instructions, comparing baselines, single suffixes, concatenated suffixes, and ensemble strategies.

image_metadata.caption: ARCA outperforms UAT and AutoPrompt at triggering toxic outputs in GPT-2/GPT-J. (Jones et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining ARCA's score decomposition technique to validate why the approximation improvement matters in practice compared to UAT and AutoPrompt baselines.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing average success rates of ARCA and baseline methods (UAT, AutoPrompt) at triggering GPT-2 and GPT-J to produce toxic outputs, broken down by output length (1, 2, 3 tokens) and full CivilComments dataset.

image_metadata.caption: Jailbreak attack types and success rates across LLMs. (Wei et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing jailbreak attacks to contrast different attack strategies and motivate why robust alignment is difficult; useful for discussing the two failure modes of LLM safety from Wei et al. 2023.
image_metadata.description: A table or chart showing various jailbreak prompt types (e.g., role-playing, prompt injection, obfuscation) and their empirical success rates against LLMs, organized by attack category and model tested.

image_metadata.caption: Adversarial writing UI with word importance highlights guiding human attackers. (Wallace et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining human-assisted adversarial attack tooling and how interpretability features (word importance) can be weaponized to help humans find model failure cases more efficiently.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of an adversarial writing interface showing top-5 model predictions on the upper left and a user-facing question editor on the lower right with words color-highlighted by their importance score to the model's prediction.

image_metadata.caption: Human red-teaming UI for lowering safety classifier scores on violent content. (Ziegler et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing Ziegler et al. 2022's approach to finding classifier failures; contrast with fully automated red-teaming to highlight the complementary role of human judgment.
image_metadata.description: A UI screenshot showing a tool where human annotators edit prompts or model completions to reduce a safety classifier's predicted probability of violent content, with probability scores displayed in real time.

image_metadata.caption: Tradeoff between attack success rate and diversity across red-team methods. (Perez et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the tradeoffs in automated red-teaming strategies; use to motivate why no single method dominates and why diversity metrics matter alongside success rate.
image_metadata.description: A scatter plot with attack success rate (% offensive responses) on the x-axis and sample diversity (self-BLEU) on the y-axis, with nodes representing different red-team generation methods (ZS, SFS, SL, BAD, RL variants) colored from blue (low offensiveness) to red (high offensiveness).

image_metadata.caption: Explore-Establish-Exploit pipeline for structured human-in-the-loop red-teaming. (Casper et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing structured red-teaming workflows; use as a framework students can apply when thinking about how to systematically probe LLM safety.
image_metadata.description: A pipeline diagram illustrating three sequential phases of human-in-the-loop red-teaming: Explore (discovering model behaviors), Establish (confirming failure modes), and Exploit (generating adversarial examples at scale).

image_metadata.caption: FLIRT attack effectiveness on diffusion models vs. baseline strategies. (Mehrabi et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when extending adversarial attack concepts from text LLMs to multimodal/image generation models, and when discussing how in-context learning can be used offensively.
image_metadata.description: A table showing attack effectiveness (percentage of prompts triggering unsafe image generations) for different FLIRT attack strategies across multiple diffusion models, with SFS as a baseline and unique prompt percentages in parentheses.

image_metadata.caption: Saddle point robust optimization: larger capacity improves adversarial decision boundaries. (Madry et al. 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the formal mathematical framing of adversarial robustness; use to connect the intuitive notion of adversarial attacks to the bi-level optimization objective.
image_metadata.description: A visualization related to the saddle point / robust optimization framework for adversarial robustness, likely showing decision boundaries or loss landscapes illustrating how model capacity affects robustness to adversarial perturbations.

image_metadata.caption: Perplexity filter bypass rates for adversarial suffix attacks. (Jain et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing defenses against adversarial attacks on LLMs, specifically to illustrate how perplexity-based filtering can detect unnatural adversarial suffixes and when it fails.
image_metadata.description: A bar chart showing 'PPL Passed' and 'PPL Window Passed' rates — the fraction of adversarial prompts (with suffixes from Zou et al. 2023) that bypass a perplexity-based filter — across different experimental conditions.

image_metadata.caption: Seven LLM patterns mapped by data proximity and performance/cost tradeoff.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of any lesson on LLM system design to orient students before diving into individual patterns.
image_metadata.description: A two-axis diagram organizing seven LLM system design patterns along two spectrums: 'closer to data' vs 'closer to user' (horizontal) and 'reducing cost/risk' vs 'improving performance' (vertical), with labeled connections between patterns.

image_metadata.caption: BERTScore uses hard alignment (left); MoverScore uses soft alignment (right).
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching the limitations of exact-match metrics and introducing embedding-based evaluation; use to explain the tradeoff between precision and semantic flexibility.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side visual comparison of BERTScore (left, showing one-to-one 'hard' token alignment between reference and generated text) and MoverScore (right, showing many-to-one 'soft' token alignment using optimal transport).

image_metadata.caption: ROUGE scores vary widely across evaluation dimensions, revealing metric limitations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the pitfalls of standard NLP metrics, especially before introducing more robust or task-specific evaluation approaches.
image_metadata.description: A multi-dimensional radar or bar chart showing how ROUGE scores vary across different evaluation dimensions, illustrating the metric's inconsistency and limited adaptability across tasks.

image_metadata.caption: RAG combines dense retrieval with a generative LLM to reduce hallucinations.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the introduction of the RAG pattern to ground students in the original architecture before discussing variants or optimizations.
image_metadata.description: An architecture diagram of Retrieval Augmented Generation (RAG) showing: a document index built with DPR encoders, a retriever fetching relevant documents at inference time, concatenation of input with retrieved documents, and a BART LLM generating output tokens.

image_metadata.caption: FiD encodes each retrieved document separately, then decodes jointly.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing RAG to illustrate an architectural variant that handles multiple retrieved documents more efficiently.
image_metadata.description: An architecture diagram of Fusion-in-Decoder (FiD) showing how multiple retrieved documents are each independently encoded (fused in the encoder) and then jointly decoded by a single generative decoder to produce the answer.

image_metadata.caption: RETRO integrates chunked retrieval throughout pre-training via cross-attention.
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing RAG variants, especially to highlight the distinction between inference-time retrieval (RAG) and pre-training-integrated retrieval (RETRO).
image_metadata.description: An architecture diagram of RETRO (Retrieval-Enhanced Transformer) showing chunked input processing, frozen BERT retriever fetching neighbors per chunk, a differentiable encoder, and chunked cross-attention integrated throughout pre-training and inference.

image_metadata.caption: RETRO-fitting achieves near-scratch RETRO performance by training <10% of weights. (Source: RETRO paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how existing pre-trained models can be upgraded with retrieval capabilities without full retraining, especially when discussing cost-performance trade-offs in RAG systems.
image_metadata.description: Bar chart comparing perplexity scores of baseline models, RETRO-fitted models, and RETRO trained from scratch, showing that RETRO-fitting (freezing pre-trained weights and training only chunked cross-attention and neighbor encoder) achieves performance close to full RETRO training.

image_metadata.caption: Internet-augmented LLMs retrieve, chunk, rank, then prompt with evidence. (Source: Internet-augmented LMs paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing RAG pipelines to students, particularly to contrast lightweight retrieval methods (TF-IDF) with dense embedding approaches, or when discussing few-shot prompting with retrieved evidence.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram showing the pipeline of internet-augmented LLMs: a query triggers a Google Search, retrieved documents are chunked into paragraphs, ranked via TF-IDF and cosine similarity, and top paragraphs are used as few-shot evidence to condition the LLM.

image_metadata.caption: RAG for CodeT5+: retrieval provides crucial library context for code generation. (Source: CodeT5+ paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing RAG applications beyond question answering, or when illustrating how retrieved context improves correctness in structured generation tasks like code.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing three evaluation settings for CodeT5+ with RAG: generative-only, retrieval-only, and retrieval-augmented generation, illustrating how retrieved code snippets are combined with the generative model for code generation tasks.

image_metadata.caption: HyDE generates a hypothetical document to bridge query-document embedding gap. (Source: HyDE paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching dense retrieval limitations (query-document mismatch) and creative solutions, or when discussing zero-shot and unsupervised retrieval methods in RAG pipelines.
image_metadata.description: Flowchart of Hypothetical Document Embeddings (HyDE): a query is fed to an LLM (e.g., InstructGPT) to generate a hypothetical document, which is then encoded by an unsupervised encoder (e.g., Contriever) to retrieve real documents via embedding similarity.

image_metadata.caption: ULMFiT: pre-train, domain fine-tune, then classifier fine-tune — foundational NLP transfer learning. (Source: ULMFiT paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a fine-tuning module to establish historical context and the three-stage transfer learning protocol before introducing BERT and GPT.
image_metadata.description: Three-stage diagram of ULMFiT: (1) LM pre-training on Wikitext-103, (2) LM fine-tuning on target domain data, and (3) classifier fine-tuning with added linear layers on the target task, using AWD-LSTM architecture.

image_metadata.caption: BERT pre-trains bidirectionally via MLM and NSP, then fine-tunes per task. (Source: BERT paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining BERT's architecture and training objectives, especially to contrast with GPT's unidirectional approach or to illustrate task-specific fine-tuning with classification heads.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing BERT's pre-training objectives (masked language modeling and next sentence prediction) and its fine-tuning on downstream tasks such as NER, Q&A, and classification, highlighting the encoder-only architecture.

image_metadata.caption: GPT pre-trains via next-token prediction, then fine-tunes with task-specific input formats. (Source: GPT paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when contrasting GPT's unidirectional, decoder-only design with BERT's bidirectional encoder, or when tracing the lineage from GPT-1 to modern instruction-tuned models.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of GPT's pre-training on BooksCorpus via next-token prediction and fine-tuning pipeline for multiple tasks (classification, entailment, similarity, Q&A) using a decoder-only Transformer architecture with task-specific input transformations.

image_metadata.caption: T5 unifies all NLP tasks as text-to-text using task-specific prefixes. (Source: T5 paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how T5 unifies NLP tasks under a single text-to-text framework, or when introducing prompt engineering as a concept that evolved from T5's prefix design.
image_metadata.description: Diagram of T5's encoder-decoder architecture showing how all NLP tasks are reformulated as text-to-text problems using task-specific prefixes (e.g., 'Translate English to German:', 'Summarize:'), with pre-training on C4 using a denoising/masked language modeling objective.

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT: SFT → reward model → PPO RLHF pipeline for instruction alignment. (Source: InstructGPT paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching RLHF and alignment techniques, or when explaining how InstructGPT/ChatGPT differ from base GPT-3 in terms of training methodology.
image_metadata.description: Three-step diagram of InstructGPT's training pipeline: (1) supervised fine-tuning on human demonstrations, (2) reward model training from human preference comparisons, and (3) PPO-based reinforcement learning from human feedback (RLHF) to align the model with instructions.

image_metadata.caption: Prefix-tuning prepends trainable vectors to all layers, matching full fine-tuning at 0.1% parameters. (Source: Prefix-Tuning paper)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when comparing PEFT methods (prefix-tuning, prompt tuning, LoRA), especially to illustrate where in the model trainable parameters are inserted and the efficiency gains achieved.
image_metadata.description: Comparison diagram of prefix-tuning versus full fine-tuning: prefix-tuning prepends trainable prefix vectors to hidden states of all Transformer layers while keeping original LM weights frozen, contrasted with updating all parameters in full fine-tuning.

image_metadata.caption: Adapter layers inserted after attention and FFN in each transformer block. (Source: Houlsby et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, specifically to contrast adapter insertion points with full fine-tuning before moving to LoRA.
image_metadata.description: Diagram showing the adapter technique architecture: two fully connected network layers inserted into each transformer block — one after the attention layer and one after the feed-forward network layer, with the rest of the transformer weights frozen.

image_metadata.caption: LoRA decomposes weight updates into two low-rank matrices A and B. (Source: Hu et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining adapter layers, when introducing LoRA as an alternative PEFT method; use to explain why low-rank updates provide implicit regularization.
image_metadata.description: Diagram illustrating LoRA's mechanism: the original pre-trained weight matrix W is frozen, and a parallel low-rank decomposition (two matrices A and B of reduced rank r) is trained and added to the forward pass output.

image_metadata.caption: QLoRA combines 4-bit quantization with LoRA adapters to reduce fine-tuning memory. (Source: Dettmers et al.)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after LoRA is understood, when discussing how to fine-tune very large models under GPU memory constraints; useful for comparing memory footprints.
image_metadata.description: Diagram comparing QLoRA to standard LoRA fine-tuning, illustrating the 4-bit quantized frozen base model, the 16-bit LoRA adapters trained on top, double quantization, and paged optimizers for memory management.

image_metadata.caption: GPTCache uses embedding similarity to serve cached LLM responses. (Source: GPTCache)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching LLM serving optimization or cost reduction strategies; use to contrast semantic caching with traditional key-value caching.
image_metadata.description: Architecture diagram of GPTCache showing the pipeline: incoming LLM requests are checked against a semantic cache using embedding similarity; cache hits return stored responses, cache misses are forwarded to the LLM and the result is stored.

image_metadata.caption: Anthropic's HHH prompt guides models toward helpful, harmless, honest responses. (Source: Anthropic)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing guardrail strategies, specifically to illustrate prompt-based control before discussing output validation approaches.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of an example HHH (Helpful, Harmless, Honest) system prompt used by Anthropic to guide model behavior, showing the structured natural-language instructions that define desired assistant properties.

image_metadata.caption: Midjourney blocks NSFW prompts with an input guardrail error message. (Source: Midjourney)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining input guardrail types; use as a real-world case study to ground the distinction between input vs. output guardrails.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of a Midjourney error message triggered when a user attempts to generate NSFW content, demonstrating an input guardrail blocking an inappropriate prompt before the model processes it.

image_metadata.caption: Microsoft's 18 Human-AI guidelines organized across the user journey. (Source: Microsoft)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching defensive UX or responsible AI product design; use as a framework students can apply when evaluating or designing LLM-powered interfaces.
image_metadata.description: A 2x2 or timeline-style framework diagram organizing Microsoft's 18 Human-AI Interaction Guidelines across phases of the user journey (initially, during interaction, when wrong, over time), with each guideline shown as a short action rule.

image_metadata.caption: Google Bard displays a disclaimer to set user expectations about AI accuracy. (Source: Google Bard)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing how to communicate model limitations to users; use to contrast short-term trust reduction with long-term trust building through transparency.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of Google Bard's UI showing a disclaimer banner warning users that Bard may display inaccurate or offensive information, with a note that the code provided will not work (nrows is not a valid argument).

image_metadata.caption: Midjourney embeds feedback signals (upscale, variation, regenerate) into its UX. (Source: Midjourney)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when teaching how to collect human preference data for fine-tuning; use to illustrate how product UX can double as a data collection mechanism.
image_metadata.description: Screenshot of Midjourney's image generation UI showing the feedback buttons: U (upscale/download), V (variation), and regenerate icons beneath generated images, illustrating how explicit user feedback is embedded in the product workflow.

image_metadata.caption: LLM patterns mapped across data-to-user and defensive-to-offensive axes. (Source: Eugene Yan)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of a lesson as a roadmap, or at the end as a synthesis activity; use to help students categorize and compare patterns they have learned.
image_metadata.description: A 2x2 matrix diagram plotting LLM design patterns on two axes: data-to-user (horizontal) and defensive-to-offensive (vertical), positioning patterns like RAG, caching, fine-tuning, guardrails, and defensive UX in their respective quadrants.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF uses human feedback as a reward signal to optimize language models. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of an RLHF lesson when introducing why standard loss functions and metrics like BLEU/ROUGE are insufficient for capturing human preferences in language generation.
image_metadata.description: A diagram illustrating the high-level concept of Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), showing how human feedback is used as a reward signal to optimize a language model beyond simple next-token prediction loss.

image_metadata.caption: Pretraining produces the base LM used to generate reward model training data. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the RLHF pipeline stages, specifically before discussing reward model training, to ground students in the prerequisite step of having a pretrained base language model.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the language model pretraining stage in the RLHF pipeline, showing how a base language model is trained before being fine-tuned with human feedback, serving as the starting point for reward model data generation.

image_metadata.caption: A reward model assigns scalar scores to text, encoding human preferences for RL training. (Source: HuggingFace Blog)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining pretraining and before introducing the RL fine-tuning stage, when students need to understand how human preferences are operationalized as a numeric reward for policy optimization.
image_metadata.description: A diagram of the reward model training stage in the RLHF pipeline, illustrating how a preference/reward model is trained to assign scalar scores to text outputs, with context on model size choices used by OpenAI, Anthropic, and DeepMind.

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: pretraining → SFT → RLHF. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an orientation map; return to it as a 'you are here' anchor when transitioning between phases
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing ChatGPT's development pipeline: pretraining on large data, supervised fine-tuning (SFT), and RLHF with a reward model and PPO optimization

image_metadata.caption: Language models generate text token-by-token via next-token prediction. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing language models to students unfamiliar with how LLMs generate text; use before explaining why completion alone is insufficient and SFT is needed
image_metadata.description: An animated GIF demonstrating autoregressive text completion by a language model, showing how a model predicts and appends the next token step-by-step given a prompt context

image_metadata.caption: RedPajama vs. LLaMA: ~1 trillion tokens across diverse data sources. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the pretraining data bottleneck or when explaining why 'garbage in, garbage out' applies to LLMs; useful for grounding abstract scale claims
image_metadata.description: A bar chart comparing dataset compositions of RedPajama and LLaMA training data, showing token counts across sources like Common Crawl, C4, GitHub, Wikipedia, Books, ArXiv, and StackExchange, with totals around 1 trillion tokens

image_metadata.caption: Training data demand is outpacing new Internet data generation. (Villalobos et al., 2022 via Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing limitations of scaling pretraining data, or to motivate why RLHF and human feedback become critical when raw data supply is constrained
image_metadata.description: A line graph showing the projected growth of training dataset size versus the growth rate of new Internet data generation over time, illustrating that dataset demand will outpace supply within a few years

image_metadata.caption: Pretrained models may complete prompts unhelpfully; SFT steers toward preferred responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the start of the SFT section to motivate why fine-tuning is needed; effective as a discussion prompt asking students which completion they would prefer and why
image_metadata.description: A diagram showing multiple possible completions for the prompt 'How to make pizza', contrasting undesirable completions (e.g., more questions, unrelated text) with the preferred instructional response, illustrating the motivation for SFT

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's 40 InstructGPT labelers: educational backgrounds vary widely. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when discussing the human element of RLHF data collection; use to spark discussion about bias, representation, and whose preferences are being optimized
image_metadata.description: A chart or infographic showing the educational background distribution of OpenAI's 40 human labelers used to create InstructGPT demonstration data, likely showing degrees in various fields

image_metadata.caption: SFT uses demonstration labels; RLHF uses a reward model to score and optimize responses. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the transition from Phase 2 (SFT) to Phase 3 (RLHF) to highlight what changes structurally; useful for students who conflate fine-tuning with reinforcement learning
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side comparison diagram contrasting the SFT training setup (prompt → demonstration response) with the RLHF training setup (prompt → model response → reward model score → policy update), highlighting the structural difference between the two phases

image_metadata.caption: Reward model loss decreases as the score gap between preferred and rejected responses grows. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the RM loss equation to provide geometric intuition; particularly useful for students with calculus/ML background who benefit from seeing loss landscape shapes
image_metadata.description: A graph visualizing the reward model loss function, showing how the loss behaves as the score difference between a preferred and a non-preferred response varies, illustrating the pairwise ranking objective

image_metadata.caption: OpenAI's labeler UI: rank responses 1–7 and by preference to train the reward model. (Huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how comparison data is collected for the reward model; use to discuss the 73% inter-labeler agreement statistic and what it implies about subjectivity in human feedback
image_metadata.description: A screenshot of the actual web UI used by OpenAI labelers to rank InstructGPT responses, showing a prompt, multiple model-generated responses, a 1–7 rating scale, and a ranking interface

image_metadata.caption: Three-phase ChatGPT training: SFT → Reward Model → RL fine-tuning. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of any RLHF lesson as an overview, and revisit when explaining the RL fine-tuning phase to anchor the KL divergence constraint in the broader pipeline.
image_metadata.description: A three-phase diagram showing the development pipeline of ChatGPT/InstructGPT: (1) Supervised Fine-Tuning (SFT), (2) Reward Model training, and (3) RL fine-tuning with the reward model, illustrating how each phase builds on the previous one.

image_metadata.caption: RLHF objective: maximize reward while penalizing KL divergence from SFT model. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce after explaining the conceptual motivation for the KL constraint; use to transition students from intuition to formal understanding before discussing the InstructGPT paper's notation.
image_metadata.description: A mathematical equation displaying the RLHF objective function, showing it as the sum of two terms: the expected reward from the reward model and a KL divergence penalty term that constrains the RL-tuned policy from straying too far from the SFT model.

image_metadata.caption: Schulman's proposed RL-based solutions to LLM hallucination. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the hallucination section, before presenting the InstructGPT empirical results, to create a productive tension between Schulman's hypothesis and the actual findings.
image_metadata.description: A screenshot from John Schulman's talk proposing solutions to fix hallucination using RL, outlining his argument that LLMs know what they know and that RLHF could in principle be used to force models to only output information they are confident about.

image_metadata.caption: InstructGPT data: RLHF worsens hallucination despite improving overall human preference. (huyenchip.com)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after Schulman's proposed fix (image 3) to highlight the gap between theory and practice, and use to spark discussion about evaluation trade-offs in LLM alignment.
image_metadata.description: A chart or figure from the InstructGPT paper showing that RLHF-trained models exhibit worse hallucination rates compared to SFT-only models, despite being preferred overall by human labelers across other quality dimensions.

image_metadata.caption: Two complementary strategies for high-quality human annotation data. (Weng 2024)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show at the very start of a lesson on human data quality to orient students before diving into specific methods; use as a running reference to locate each new technique within the overall framework.
image_metadata.description: A two-branch diagram showing two complementary approaches to achieving high data quality: one branch focused on improving rater quality (e.g., better annotators, training, guidelines) and another on aggregation/modeling techniques (e.g., majority vote, weighted aggregation, learned models).

image_metadata.caption: Quality assurance as a feedback loop over annotation quality attributes. (Daniel et al. 2018)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when discussing how to operationalize data quality standards; useful before explaining specific QA mechanisms like inter-annotator agreement or rater audits.
image_metadata.description: A structured diagram illustrating the quality assurance process for crowdsourced annotation, showing how quality attributes feed into a quality model, which in turn drives quality assurance actions that loop back to improve the annotation process.

image_metadata.caption: Non-expert AMT workers match experts in MT evaluation when spammers are down-weighted. (Callison-Burch 2009)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing the wisdom-of-the-crowd concept and when discussing how to handle low-quality annotators; pairs well with explaining weighting schemes for aggregating noisy labels.
image_metadata.description: Two-panel figure: (Left) Bar chart comparing expert-expert vs. expert-non-expert agreement rates on MT ranking tasks under different weighting schemes (uniform, weighted by experts, weighted by non-experts), with a 1/3 chance baseline marked. (Right) Bar chart comparing BLEU scores of translations from AMT non-experts vs. LCD professional translators across multiple MT systems.

image_metadata.caption: Expert–non-expert annotation agreement varies substantially across conversation topics. (Wang et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Use when discussing sources of rater disagreement and the descriptive annotation paradigm; effective for motivating why demographic and topic metadata should be collected alongside labels.
image_metadata.description: A heatmap or scatter/bar chart showing Pearson or Spearman correlation coefficients between non-expert crowdworker annotations and expert (Trust & Safety professional) annotations, broken down by conversation topic, revealing high variance across topics.

image_metadata.caption: Taxonomy of root causes for rater disagreement in human annotation. (Zhang et al. 2023)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce at the start of the rater disagreement section; revisit when explaining disagreement deconvolution and the descriptive vs. prescriptive annotation paradigms.
image_metadata.description: A hierarchical taxonomy tree diagram categorizing causes of rater disagreement into major branches such as task/item difficulty, annotator characteristics (identity, knowledge, values), and stochastic/random errors, with leaf-level examples for each.

image_metadata.caption: Four architectures for learning from multi-annotator labels on a shared BERT encoder. (Davani et al. 2021)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when explaining how to model annotator disagreement computationally; use to motivate the multi-task architecture before presenting its F1 and uncertainty estimation results.
image_metadata.description: Side-by-side architecture diagrams comparing four model setups for handling multi-annotator labels: (1) single model trained on majority vote, (2) single model trained on individual labels, (3) multi-task model with one head per annotator sharing a common encoder, and (4) a variant thereof — all built on a shared BERT backbone.

image_metadata.caption: Jury learning aggregates predictions from demographically specified annotator 'jurors.' (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from passive disagreement modeling to active jury composition; effective for discussions on fairness and whose values a model should reflect.
image_metadata.description: A conceptual illustration of the jury learning framework: individual annotators with demographic characteristics are treated as 'jurors'; a practitioner specifies a jury composition; the model samples predictions from each juror and aggregates them into a final verdict.

image_metadata.caption: DCN architecture jointly embeds comment text, annotator identity, and demographics for jury learning. (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after introducing the jury learning concept when students need to understand the technical implementation; useful for students with a deep learning background who want architectural details.
image_metadata.description: A detailed neural network architecture diagram of the Deep & Cross Network (DCN) used for jury learning, showing parallel input streams for comment text (processed by a pre-trained language model), annotator embedding, and group/demographic embedding, which are combined through cross and deep layers before a final prediction head.

image_metadata.caption: Jury composition significantly shifts model predictions compared to an annotator-agnostic baseline. (Gordon et al. 2022)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Present after explaining the jury learning architecture to show its practical impact; effective for motivating discussions on fairness and representation in annotation pipelines.
image_metadata.description: Bar or line charts comparing jury learning against an annotator-agnostic baseline across multiple jury compositions (e.g., all-male, all-female, balanced, random), showing differences in predicted toxicity rates or F1 scores, demonstrating that jury composition meaningfully shifts model outputs.

image_metadata.caption: Influence function estimates closely match leave-one-out retraining on MNIST. (Kohn & Liang 2017)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show when introducing influence functions as a data quality tool; use to justify why the approximation is trustworthy before discussing its application to detecting mislabeled or low-quality training examples.
image_metadata.description: A scatter plot comparing influence function estimates of the effect of removing each training point against actual leave-one-out retraining results on 10-class MNIST, showing strong linear correlation and validating the closed-form approximation.

image_metadata.caption: Data map of SNLI training set partitioned by confidence and variability. (Swayamdipta et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce this when explaining the Data Maps method (Swayamdipta et al. 2020) — show it to anchor the abstract concepts of 'confidence' and 'variability' to a concrete visual partition of a real dataset before discussing how these regions relate to data quality.
image_metadata.description: A 2D scatter plot (data map) of the SNLI training set using a RoBERTa classifier, with axes for 'variability' (x-axis) and 'confidence' (y-axis). Points are colored by region: easy-to-learn (high confidence, low variability), ambiguous (high variability), and hard-to-learn (low confidence, low variability). Density contours show clustering of samples in each region.

image_metadata.caption: Label-flipped points shift to low-confidence regions, validating data map mislabel detection. (Swayamdipta et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show immediately after the data map figure (Image 1) when explaining how the hard-to-learn region relates to mislabeling — use it as empirical validation of the Data Maps hypothesis before moving to other detection methods.
image_metadata.description: A side-by-side or overlaid data map scatter plot showing how data points shift in the confidence-variability space after their labels are artificially flipped. Originally high-confidence, low-variability (easy-to-learn) points migrate toward low-confidence, slightly higher-variability regions after label flipping, visually confirming that mislabeled samples appear in the hard-to-learn region.

image_metadata.caption: Threshold samples calibrate AUM cutoff to separate mislabeled from clean data. (Pleiss et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when explaining the AUM method and the threshold-sample trick — show it after defining the margin formula to make the threshold-setting mechanism concrete before presenting experimental results.
image_metadata.description: A diagram or plot illustrating how artificially inserted 'threshold samples' (with known incorrect labels) are used in the Area Under the Margin (AUM) method to set a decision boundary separating mislabeled from correctly labeled samples. Shows AUM score distributions for clean, mislabeled, and threshold samples, demonstrating that threshold samples help calibrate the cutoff.

image_metadata.caption: AUM filtering vs. other methods on CIFAR-10/100 with varying label noise rates. (Pleiss et al. 2020)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Show after explaining both AUM and NCV methods — use it to compare methods empirically and prompt discussion about which approach is most practical and why performance varies with noise rate.
image_metadata.description: A bar or line chart comparing test error on CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 benchmarks under varying levels of randomly mislabeled training data, contrasting AUM-based data filtering against other methods such as NCV, MentorNet, and standard training with noisy labels.

image_metadata.caption: INCV algorithm iteratively removes mislabeled samples via cross-validated label agreement. (Chen et al. 2019)
image_metadata.when_to_show: Introduce when transitioning from NCV to INCV — show it to make the iterative refinement process concrete, especially when students ask how the method handles high noise rates where a single pass is insufficient.
image_metadata.description: A pseudocode or flowchart of the INCV (Iterative Noisy Cross-Validation) algorithm, showing the iterative process: splitting data into halves, training on each half, identifying 'clean' samples whose labels match cross-validated predictions, removing likely noisy samples, and repeating until convergence.
